I see. This is why I wanted to decouple as much as possible from `mocket` internals and just rely on the `socket` package patching.

The problem here is that `pook` matching engine is not triggered, the current implementation is an one-way flow only. The interceptors needs to trigger the matcher engine, such as:
https://github.com/h2non/pook/blob/master/pook/interceptors/urllib3.py#L90-L91

All the mock matching and counting logic is agnostic to the interceptor, but the interceptor is the entry point for this that notifies higher layers. Clearly, the matching engine is useless from the `mocket` perspective, since it would be already implemented there.

Honestly, I don't see how you can implement that in `mocket` without adding some hack somewhere. There should be a callback-like mechanism that should trigger the mock matching engine in order the trigger the required logic in `pook` side. 

I was thinking about registering a custom `Entry` object via mocket that inject a `pook` dependency. Then, we a request is matched and mocked out in `mocket`, a new logic should be added in `mocket` in order to infer if the `Entry` object was `pook` originated, and then trigger the matcher engine. It should be simple to achieve, but ideally I was thinking that integration should be frictionless in both ends without too much ad-hoc logic.

Does this sounds good to you? Do you have a better, alternative idea?

I should have clarified it better in the docs.

When you create a [mako template lookup object](http://docs.makotemplates.org/en/latest/usage.html#using-templatelookup) (or just a [Template object](http://docs.makotemplates.org/en/latest/usage.html#basic-usage)) with a plim preprocessor, Mako uses that preprocessor for preparing mako markup from every single template involved in rendering the final output (i.e. parent files, included files etc.) There is no way, as far as I know, to turn off the preprocessor for some specific files we'd like to treat as plain mako markup that do not require preprocessing.

Unfortunately, Plim itself does not provide transparent compatibility with plain Mako markup. It only supports "row-level tolerance", i.e. you are allowed [to start a line](http://plim.readthedocs.org/en/latest/en/syntax.html#raw-html-tags) with a plain HTML tag, or any Mako tag that looks like a html tag (basically, Plim refuses to parse a line that starts with the ``<`` character and outputs it "as is").

As for ``#`` in [new-style python blocks](http://plim.readthedocs.org/en/latest/en/syntax.html#new-style-blocks), the reason why the comment appears in mako markup is precisely because it is allowed to put Python code right after the block indicator ``---``:
```
--- x = 1
```
and
```
-------------
    x = 1
```
So, if you put ``# my comment`` instead of `` x = 1`` right after the ``---``, it will appear in the resulting mako template.

There is a solution to this, which is to embrace the GLib (ew, I know): https://github.com/nathan-hoad/gbulb

If you want to coordinate among binding and library authors, please (encourage them all to) contact dbus@lists.freedesktop.org.

> Implementing higher-level, per-interface wrappers should not be necessary.

I can understand the appeal of thinking this, because I used to think the same: who needs domain-specific C/Python/whatever APIs when you can just provide a D-Bus API and use each language's generic bindings?

In my experience, the answer turns out to be that this results in a lot of sharp edges, and an API that can't be used in practice without at least some understanding of D-Bus. You can have a convenient high-level API for some particular domain/problem that can be used without understanding D-Bus, or you can have generic bindings for D-Bus, but I have yet to encounter anything that achieves both.

> Looks like the DBus has standarized on '\0'-terminated binary strings

I think the way to think about this is: it's a common convention, and ideally that common convention would be easy, but it can't be done at the expense of the other possible uses for byte-arrays. Not every byte-array is a bytestring.

Saying that D-Bus has standardized on the `ay` type containing `'\0'`-terminated strings is like saying that Unix has standardized on files containing lines of text with `\n` at the end of each line, or saying that Linux has standardized on filenames being UTF-8. We can encourage that, we can make it the default in new designs, and we can optimize APIs for it; but if Python didn't allow reading binary files, or didn't let you distinguish between a text file that has `\n` as its last byte and a text file that doesn't, or didn't let you delete a file whose name isn't UTF-8, you'd be right to complain and consider it to be a bug.

In fact I'm fairly sure the reason Flatpak is using `ay` instead of `s` is because of exactly the invalid assumptions that I mentioned. If we could assume that Unix filenames were UTF-8 and streams were text, then we could have APIs like `RunCommand(s: command, s: stdin) → i: exit_status, s: stdout, s: stderr` and everything would be simple: but filenames (and hence commands) aren't necessarily UTF-8, and streams aren't necessarily text, so a fully-general API to run commands is more like `RunCommand(ay: command_plus_0, ay: stdin) → i: exit_status, ay: stdout, ay: stderr`.

While doing so, I _think_, I may have found a related issue. I saw that when decoding `u`/`U` values, in our version we silently convert those to a new value. This is an example:

```python
In [6]: u = ulid.from_str('01BX73KC0TNH409RTFD1UXKM00')

In [7]: u
Out[7]: <ULID('01BX73KC0TNH409RTFD3ZXKM00')>

In [8]: u.str
Out[8]: '01BX73KC0TNH409RTFD3ZXKM00'
```
`1U` is silently converted to `3Z`.

I checked  [Crockford's Base32](http://crockford.com/wrmg/base32.html) and values `u`/`U` are regarded as (and I quote) an _Accidental obscenity_... 

I'm guessing we should error out if we encounter those? I checked the [Java implementation](https://github.com/huxi/sulky/blob/master/sulky-ulid/src/main/java/de/huxhorn/sulky/ulid/ULID.java#L361) we're currently using and we do get an `IllegalArgumentException` for those cases ([this hilarious test](https://github.com/huxi/sulky/blob/master/sulky-ulid/src/test/groovy/de/huxhorn/sulky/ulid/ULIDSpec.groovy#L545) reflects it as well):

(Currently they don't call the gc hooks either, but I've been debating changing that, and with the addition of `finalize` I'm leaning towards making the switch.) Yes. Undocumented for now.

One thing I'm just not sure about is how this function should actually work--meaning the underlying semantics as well as inputs.  Questions arise:

1.  Should it operate on tasks (created by spawn()) or simply a collection of coroutines?  If the latter, I assume that gather() would implicitly spawn them into tasks.

2. How does error handling work?  If one of the tasks fails, do all of the remaining tasks get cancelled?  

3. How does cancellation work?  Would it cancel only the gather() operation or does it propagate and cancel all of the tasks being watched?

4. Could gather() return partial results.  For example, if there was a timeout, could it return results gathered so far, but then be called again on the remaining tasks to get their results?

5. Do results have to come back in order?

Initially, I thought it might make sense to simply copy what asyncio was doing.  However, I'm now no longer sure.  So, with all of this said, I'd be really interest to get thoughts about how a gather() function should work.   The idea of a race() or first() function would be interesting as well.  It would have some of the same issues (e.g., do tasks that haven't finished get cancelled?).

I've tweaked UniversalQueue to work across kernels running in different threads (it can be done using a bit of thread-local storage).    I've also modified Curio to not allow multiple kernels to run on a single thread at once.

For whatever it's worth, I got to thinking about the problem of running multiple kernels in multiple threads.  I tried this experiment involving sockets and it worked fine::

    from curio import *
    from curio.socket import *

    async def tcp_server(label, sock, handle_client):
        print('Server', label, 'starting')
        while True:
            client, addr = await sock.accept()
            print(label, 'Connection from:', addr)
            await spawn(handle_client(client, addr))

    async def echo_client(client, addr):
        async with client:
            while True:
                data = await client.recv(10000)
                if not data:
                    break
                await client.sendall(b'Got:' + data)
        print('Connection closed')

    import threading

    def main(addr, nkernels):
        sock = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.bind(addr)
        sock.listen(1)

        for n in range(nkernels):
            threading.Thread(target=run, args=(tcp_server(n, sock, echo_client),)).start()

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        main(('', 25000), 4)

I've used a similar pattern with threads and processes in the past.  So, I suppose it shouldn't be a huge surprise that it would work okay.  Still neat.

This is quite easy, although not documented unfortunately. If there exists a file in the directory of the .core file, this will be used instead of the one from the original repo. So these steps should hopefully work
1. Copy the original or1200 core file to a new directory
2. Add the path to this directory at the end of cores_root in fusesoc.conf (or add --cores-root=/path/to/or1200 to the command-line)
3. Add your new/modified files to the new directory with the correct subdirectories (e.g. rtl/verilog/newfile.v)
4. Add the new files to the .core file

Done! Out of curiousity, is there a reason why you want to extend the old or1200? Generally we recommend using [mor1kx](https://github.com/openrisc/mor1kx/) as that is better maintained and has more features

You need to create an `Environment` for the `Form` and an `UnsafeCommand` for the `TextField`. Check out https://jeltef.github.io/PyLaTeX/current/examples/own_commands_ex.html.

```python
from pylatex import Document, Package, UnsafeCommand
from pylatex.base_classes import Environment, Options


class FormEnvironment(Environment):
    """\begin{Form} ... \end{Form}"""
    _latex_name = 'Form'
    packages = [Package('hyperref')]
    escape = False
    content_separator = '\n'


doc = Document()

with doc.create(FormEnvironment()):
    # \TextField[name=multilinetextbox,multiline=true,width=\linewidth,height=1in]{}
    input_field = UnsafeCommand(
        'TextField',
        arguments=[''],
        options=Options(
            name='multilinetextbox',
            multiline='true',
            width=r'\linewidth',
            height='1in')
    )
    doc.append(input_field)

doc.generate_pdf('textfield_test', compiler='pdflatex', clean_tex=False)
```

I understand better now. I see that the issue is due to the use of pyinstaller. I only realized this after having recently encountered a similar situation in py2exe.

I believe the issue is that keyring no longer eagerly imports all of the backends, so pyinstaller can't detect the use of the backends modules, and so fails to include it in the bundle.

According to [the docs](http://www.pyinstaller.org/export/develop/project/doc/Manual.html#listing-hidden-imports), it should be possible to create a hook for pyinstaller to work with these later versions of keyring.

In the aforementioned changeset, I've added a pyinstaller hook that _should_ correct the issue. Can someone try the hook according to the docs on using a private hook? If that works, I'll submit it upstream to pyinstaller.

We have a large system that routinely issues events as parts of the system go offline.  Occasionally these same parts of the system come back online.   When this happens, we'd like to clear the associated offline event.

We're looking to use a rules engine to track the state of these events.  The idea being that we'd have a general rule for an offline event which would create a timed rule for the associated online event (which would either expire or fire if the offline state becomes online).  

Granted this can all be done directly in Python, but we're looking for a solution that can further expanded to handle more sophisticated scenarios (where multiple system events can be correlated to a single, higher level event).   

I suppose one approached would be to simply have an offline rule and an online rule and manage set of offline points outside of the engine.  The timeout logic would need to be implemented as well.

I've investigated the issue a bit more, here are my thoughts:

First of all, this issue is not about converting the whole capnp data structure into native python types, but about "shallowly" converting to a dict, so nested struct is certainly not considered. Generally, I think such kind of thing cannot and should not be done. For example:
```python
Object = namedtuple('Object', ['dimension', 'weight'])
Dimension = namedtuple('Dimension', ['x', 'y', 'z'])
o = Object(Dimension(10, 15, 20), 50)
o._asdict()
```
Will the nested `Dimension` be converted? No. But the user can always choose to do it himself. Another example:
```python
from types import MappingProxyType
d = {'nested': {'a': 1}, 'b': 2}
m = MappingProxyType(d)
```
Will `m['nested']` become `MappingProxyType`? No. But the user can choose to do it with one more line. Also note that `namedtuple._asdict()` is indifferent to what the type of the field is, it can be a cffi pointer or whatever.

Secondly, I don't see how `*_extended.py` can solve this issue easily. My schema has ~30 fields. Maybe I missed it, I couldn't find a way to get/iterate the field names easily. So to write equivalent methods in `*_extended.py`, I have to list all the fields manually, this is unacceptable, given that I have already written a `.capnp` file containing all the relevant information. 

Handling data consists of (possibly nested) dict/list of primitive types should cover at least 90% usage of a serialization library (which is quite a conservative figure, I would say). I think an api as succinct as possilbe should be provided for such usage. In our app, the serialization layer has a fixed api: `dict` <-> `bytes`, while the serialization lib can be changed. We have tried quite a few libs, most can do the job in one or two line.

I closed it because I thought we had reached a meeting of the minds about how to proceed and no further discussion was needed. The plan as I see it is given below.  Please let me know if you see any problems with it:

1. **Soon.** Correct the behavior of both `@a` and `@b` to create only one instance for the main task group. Both require an `@s` entry, both set instances relative to the `@s` entry and both are active if the task group has any unfinished tasks.
2. **Later.** Add `&s`, `&a` and `&b` elements for `@j` entries. Both `&a` and `&b` require `&s` and both set triggers for datetimes relative to the datetime specified by `&s`, thus mirroring the current usage for `@s`, `@a` and `@b`. 

     * These triggers are active whether or not the relevant job has unfinished prerequisites but are not active if the relevant job has been completed. 

     * The entry for `&s` supports fuzzy parsing, including relative datetimes, so that, e.g., `&s 5p -7` would  represent "5pm  7 days *before* the date specified in the `@s` entry" if @s is provided and "7 days *before* today" otherwise. I.e., relative datetimes are relative to `@s` if it is given and relative to the current datetime otherwise.  

    * Jobs without an `&s` entry implicitly share the `@s` entry, if there is one.

    * Repetition for the group task is supported using `@r` for the task group itself. This provides repetition for component jobs as well provided that relative datetimes are used in any &s entries. 
3. **Still Later.** Add `&x` "handles" to `@j` entries and change the behavior of `&q` to require a comma separated list of "handles" of immediate prerequisites.

A process only needs the CAP_NET_ADMIN capability to create TUN/TAP devices (see [capabilities(7)](http://man.cx/capabilities(7))). However to grant such capability to a process you will need admin rights on the system. The simplest way to do that is to use [setcap(8)](http://man.cx/setcap(8)) on the interpreter executable (you need to be root to do that):
```shell
setcap cap_net_admin=+pe /path/to/the/python/interpreter
```
Once done, you will be able to create TUN/TAP device as a normal user using the modified interpreter executable.

PS: you shouldn't modify the interpreter provided with your OS but instead create a virtualenv with --always-copy and execute the previous command on the interpreter created in your virtualenv

These are UDP/IP, and use a much simpler encapsulation -- but initiating the "Connected" session is much more complex, and requires the Forward Open request via an Explicit / Unconnected session, which then establishes the Implicit / Connected session... 

All this only works within the same LAN, of course, because the receiving party (the EP1 device) has to be able to directly send UDP/IP packets to the initiating party (your Python program).  So, they all have to be in the same Layer 2 IP network, usually.

this can be done with the `--pose_relation` argument (default: `translation_part`). There are three pose relation arguments for the rotation error:

* `angle_rad`: angle in radians
* `angle_deg`: angle in degrees
* `rot_part`: unitless, uses a matrix norm for the rotation matrix part of the pose

Formulas for this are documented in the Jupyter notebook: https://github.com/MichaelGrupp/evo/blob/master/notebooks/metrics.py_API_Documentation.ipynb

An interesting mathematical property of these rotation metrics is that the values only differ by scale :)

Hmm yeah looks like it's related to how tempfiles work on Windows. The `tempfile.TemporaryFile` documentation says this:

> The returned object is a true file object on POSIX platforms. On other platforms, it is a file-like object whose file attribute is the underlying true file object. This file-like object can be used in a with statement, just like a normal file.

There's also a `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile` that behaves more consistently across different platforms, and the returned file-like object has a `file` attribute that is the real open file, so we should be able to use that. I just need to get numpy installed on Windows so I can test this...

Thank you!  That was a silly bug, alright.

> I still don't see why the definition of from_spherical_coords isn't just a convention.

It *is* just a convention, but it's a convention that already exists and is in use — and not just in my code, or even code that depends on my code.  In the case of a SWSH, those functions already exist and were given their conventional values [in 1966](http://aip.scitation.org/doi/abs/10.1063/1.1931221), and their relationship to Wigner D matrices [in 1967](http://aip.scitation.org/doi/abs/10.1063/1.1705135).  If you plug in *your* version of this quaternion, you'll get the conventional value times some complex phase at almost every point on the sphere.  Of course, that's just one place that the basis vectors are used; they're also used throughout physics, so any time they're needed they can be computed with this function.

> Do you plan to built quaternion into numpy in the future?

I doubt that there's any interest in this on the numpy-developers' side, so not likely.  Python is flexible enough that a package like this can insert itself into numpy, and that's probably good enough.

> I noticed SWSH is about 50% slower than Wigner_D_element, but I thought they are almost the same thing? And somehow the fastest examples shown in the test/ScratchPaper.ipynb is twice as fast as when I timed it on my laptop and clusters. Should one use the jitted inner functions to get that performance then?

Other than overhead, there shouldn't be any significant difference in speeds for these calculations.  So if you're seeing a difference, my guess is that you're calling them on different amounts of data.  For example, if you call `SWSH` a thousand times on one quaternion at a time, it will be far slower than if you call it once on a thousand quaternions.  The reason is that most of the work being done in the former case is in python (which is slow), and is just doing things like checking arguments even though those arguments may be the same each time you call it.  If your use case requires that you call many times on small sets of data, then using the jitted functions directly would probably help a little.  But in any case, if performance is a problem, you need to profile your code.

All that being said, if you need very large `ell` values and/or truly fast calculations of SWSHs or Wigner D matrices, there are better packages out there.  I've designed my code for flexibility because I have an unusual need — to evaluate SWSHs at arbitrary points under weird Lorentz transformations.  But most CMB people, for example, will probably want to use something like [the `spinsfast` package](https://github.com/moble/spinsfast).  That uses different computational techniques for much better speed and accuracy at large `ell`.

Thanks a lot, I now understand what was the exact problem (I tried by myself but I couldn't sort out a solution without breaking everything). You're right some of the fields have to be dictionaries... so creating "fake messages" can be quite cumbersome: I would have to build my messages with explicit instanciation of each sub structure (User, Chat...) just to be sure... overkill for a unittest helper. What I really needed was a convenient way to simply create Teleport like data structure using keyword arg syntax and automatic checking of the existence of the provided keys in the reference Teleport structure... telepot.namedtuple provide me the structure... so I need a workaround...

So with this brand new understanding of the situation I managed to find a solution that fits my problem well (without altering telepot's code) :
```python
def FakeMessage(**kwargs):
    """
    Helper pseudo type to create fake Telegram messages.

    Returns:
        A dict compatible with Telegram definition of a message.
    """
    # Preconditions
    assert all([key in Message._fields for key in kwargs])
    assert 'from_' in kwargs

    # Work around the fact from is a reserved word and can't be used as
    # parameter name
    kwargs['from'] = kwargs['from_']
    del kwargs['from_']

    return kwargs

def FakeChat(**kwargs):
    """
    Helper pseudo type to create fake Telegram chat.

    Returns:
        A dict compatible with Telegram definition of a message.
    """
    # Preconditions
    assert all([key in Chat._fields for key in kwargs])
    assert 'id' not in kwargs

    # KISS way to have a unique id for each chat
    kwargs['id'] = hash(time.time())

    return kwargs

def FakeUser(**kwargs):
    """
    Helper pseudo type to create fake Telegram user.

    Returns:
        A dict compatible with Telegram definition of a message.
    """
    # Preconditions
    assert all([key in User._fields for key in kwargs])
    assert 'first_name' in kwargs
    assert 'id' not in kwargs

    kwargs['id'] = hash(kwargs['first_name'])

    return kwargs

msg = FakeMessage(
    chat=FakeChat(type='text'),
    from_=FakeUser(
        first_name='Joey',
        last_name='Tribbiani',
        username='@friendsjoey'),
    text=txt)
```
This doesn't really use the namedtuple but they provide the "reference" I need to be sure not to create awful Frankenstein monsters by mistake while testing my bot. And this even gives me a good place to put my "dummy id" generation code.

Independently from this very specific problem of mine (now solved) I think the change you proposed to enable the creation of Message the same way the other namedtuple could be created would really be a real improvement to telepot.

For general help on using the lib, it's usually best to pop into the [official discord.py support server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ). Github tickets really should be for bugs, problems with code, api coverage or feature enhancements specifically to do with the code of the library. 

When you ask for help, don't forget to include info like:

- What python version are you using
- What discord.py version are you using
- What platform are you on

This helps us give more accurate advice and can sometimes help narrow down the cause of some issues that might come up.

Looking at your code, there's a few things you could improve, but there's nothing specific that seems to be jumping out as to why it's stopping at a certain number of members other than perhaps that's all the members there are. You haven't actually told us enough info to help further:

- Did you check if the loop has completed by perhaps doing a `print` before `Players = ntmp`, as if your only symptom of an issue is "It's not sending the last message", it's not enough to actually see if it's ending the loop and failing on Players, or if the loop is where the failure is.
- If it's failing in the loop, is it always a specific member.
- Is there any error traceback or is it just silent.

There's additional improvements on your code you can make also:
- You should look into [client.get_all_members()](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.Client.get_all_members) as it's getting the full member list already for you in an easy to use generator.
- If you're just adding members into the list `ntmp` that aren't already there, you can instead make `ntmp` a `set` and update the set with the data from get_all_members:
```py
# ntmp is a set
all_members = list(client.get_all_members())
ntmp.update(all_members)
```
- I see you're attempting to parse your own args, so it might be worth looking into the commands extension for discord.py. It's already in your lib, and allows you to more easily create commands and parse the arguments, including automatic type conversions, such a discord objects like member, etc. Lots of bonus' with it, and people will be willing to tell you more in the support server. [Here's an example basic bot using the commands extension](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/async/examples/basic_bot.py).

For this specific issue though, if you can do some more detailed troubleshooting and error details, and then pop into the discord.py support server to follow-up, we might be able to help a bit more.

Here's a simple test, which shows how Python doesn't validate certificates by default.

test.py:
```
try:
    from urllib.request import urlopen # python 3
    pass
except ImportError:
    from urllib2 import urlopen # python 2
    pass

url1 = "https://www.paypal.com/"
url2 = "https://23.223.25.82/" # dig www.paypal.com

for url in url1, url2:
    try:
        resp = urlopen(url)
    except:
        print("Failure WITHOUT ssl context: {0}".format(url))

import ssl
for url in url1, url2:
    try:
        resp = urlopen(url, context=ssl.create_default_context())
    except ssl.CertificateError:
        print("Certificate failure WITH ssl context: {0}".format(url))

```
Now run the code. This was tested on Ubuntu 14.04:
```
$ python3 --version
Python 3.4.3
$ python3 test.py 
Certificate failure WITH ssl context: https://23.223.25.82
```

I've encountered this same issue for about the last two years, except for the fact that the `pyodbc.so` built around 2013 still do not segfault while newer ones after do (from around 2015 onwards; in my case it may be due to changes made in Ubuntu 14.04).  All versions rebuilt against whatever system level changes (probably with virtuoso and/or other packages) resulted in this broken shared object. A workaround is to clone the [virtuoso-opensource](https://github.com/openlink/virtuoso-opensource) repository and build that manually, and modify the `Driver` key in the `VOS` section in the `odbc.ini` file to the locally compiled `virtodbc.so` file.  Example:

```
[VOS]
Driver = /home/build/virtuoso-opensource/binsrc/driver/.libs/virtodbc.so
...
```

Doing this eliminated the segmentation fault issue, even though the compiled version was the `stable/7` branch talking to a server running Virtuoso 6.1.6; this was done due to compilation error while trying to build using that tag.  Might be why this ultimately segfaulted, maybe.

I have been looking into this, but I don't have a solution just yet.  Here are some notes and links, but I haven't been able to make anything work.

The problem occurs with any character greater than 0xFFFF, which this emoji is (0x1_F1E7).  Windows and SQL Server started out using UCS2, which could not encode this character, but as of 2008 I thought were actually using UTF16-LE.

This article has good information: [Collation and Unicode Support | Microsoft Docs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/collations/collation-and-unicode-support)

"To use the UTF-16 collations available in SQL Server 2017, you can select one of the supplementary characters _SC collations (Windows collations only) to improve searching and sorting of some Unicode characters." 

"Introduced in SQL Server 2012, a new family of supplementary character (SC) collations can be used with the data types nchar, nvarchar and sql_variant. For example: Latin1_General_100_CI_AS_SC"

It sounds like the minimum requirements may be:

* SQL Server 2012
* ODBC 3.7
* Use a collation ending with "_SC" (supplemental characters)

Also, "[s]upplementary characters are not supported for use in metadata, such as in names of database objects", so no emojis can be used as column names. 

Reference:

* [MS - Supplementary Characters](https://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms180942(v=sql.105).aspx)
* [Supplementary Characters in SQL Server](https://devio.wordpress.com/2012/05/13/supplementary-characters-in-sql-server/)

Thanks a lot for providing a link to the issue discussion and for suggesting a workaround. I am a bit shocked to read that Microsoft considers this behavior 'by design'. 

> SQL Server fills that output buffer until it hits an internal limit and cannot continue to process more result sets

This is where it would be appropriate to throw on buffer overflow, or to throw on timeout (while waiting for client to consume from output buffer). Instead, the API designers chose undetermined behavior that makes the least sense, namely:

> Microsoft SQL Server may stop processing the batch before all statements in the batch are executed.

Order is not guaranteed without an ORDER BY clause on your SELECT.  Every time you call SELECT without an ORDER BY clause, the records could be returned in a different order.  This is normal SQL.  By the way, in SQL, there's no such thing as inserting in order, records are just inserted.

To get those records in a specific order, use the following SQL:
```sql
SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY name
```

@jamiebull1 ,
You may have convinced me to go for the single big class with all the functions and variables for IDF. I like the code in your link to single IDF class

Some thoughts:

### Single class
When you have a big class with a lot of functionality it can be intimidating to make changes to it

### Mixins
I don't like mixins unless there is an explicit need that is unavoidable. I find them a little opaque when used liberally.

I have been thinking about this and want to articulate some of my thoughts

For the lack of a better word, I am going to define a concept -> "Constuctability"

### Constructability
Ease of being able to build or construct something. 

**External Constructablity** 
Ease of building something external with the code. Right now "eppy" has good *external constructability* to build and manipulate IDF files

**Internal Constructablity**
Ease of building withing the code. Or developing the code further. For those of us who are working on the code, *internal constructability* is vital as it makes our work easier. 
I was using child classes IDF0->IDF1->IDF2 as an easy way of building things. As I make a child class, I only make the changes i am interested in and override only those functions (or make new functions, variables). Clearly this has resulted in code being hard to read :-(

One possibility develop using a child class. Once it is well tested, reffactor the code back into the single class

The short answer is it isn't supported, and probably won't be until something like PIL supports SVG.

The problem is that an SVG image is stored in PowerPoint as two different formats, so we'd need to convert SVG to PNG, something I've been unable to find support for in a Python library.

PowerPoint didn't support SVG until very recently, and many folks are still running one of those older versions, so an SVG image is inserted with a so-called "fallback", which is basically "here's an SVG, but if you don't understand those, here's a PNG instead".

So a general-case solution would need to insert the SVG, which is probably pretty easy, but also convert the SVG to a PNG and insert that as a fallback, all of which would be hard.

On Linux, *pynput* uses *X* to control the keyboard.

When creating an *X* connection, the target display/session is specified with the `$DISPLAY` environment variable. When you run your script directly from the Pi, it will be set correctly since the terminal was launched from an X session, but when connecting through *SSH* it will not.

Get the value of `$DISPLAY` when you run the script locally on the Pi, and set it when running the script remotely:
```
$ ssh pi.local
$ DISPLAY=":0" python my-script.py
```

If you intend to pass keystrokes from your laptop to the Pi, please note the following:
*  **You will only be able to control an *X* session; you cannot emulate actual hardware presses.** If you intent to control an application running in an *X* session, this is not a problem.
*  **Configuring *X* does not appear to be trivial.** You will need to connect remotely to *X*.

A simple, untested, script to pass keystrokes is:
```python
import os
from pynput import keyboard

def on_press(key):
    controller.press(key)

def on_release(key):
    controller.release(key)

with keyboard.Listener(
        on_press=on_press,
        on_release=on_release) as listener:
    # Specify a different target display
    os.environ['DISPLAY'] = os.environ['REMOTE_DISPLAY']
    controller = keyboard.Controller()
    listener.join()
```

Conjecture is meant to be a library that you can interface with or use directly from C to do something similar to what Hypothesis does in any language. It will probably become an optional future backend for Hypothesis.

My inclination is that a server mode for Hypothesis is going to end up being strictly worse than using Conjecture or writing tests from Python. You're going to run into a lot of the same issues that resulted in Conjecture having a radically different API from Hypothesis, and you're not going to be able to use a lot of the tools for strategy composition that work well from within Python.

If you still want to have a go at working on it, I'm very happy to help you out and offer you advice, but this is probably going to be a lot of work and I think you're better off prototyping something you're happy with on top of Hypothesis rather than trying to get it into Hypothesis itself. Once you've got that we can talk about how to either merge it into Hypothesis itself or provide you a stable API with the points that you need to build on top of (something akin to the state object you propose).

So after mulling this around a bit more this is what is actually happening:

1. fnmatch and glob will share dot logic.  By default, both will disallow `.` to be targeted by special patterns such as `*`.

2. All libraries that support `DOTGLOB` will allow the flag `DOTGLOB` or `DOTMATCH` as some may be familiar with one for glob or fnmatch, but to the underlying library, there is no difference.  Both aliases will set the same flag. There will be no `PERIOD` or `NODOTGLOB`.

3. Like `DOTGLOB`, all libraries that support `EXTGLOB` will allow the flag `EXTGLOB` or `EXTMATCH`.  `EXTMATCH` is usually associated with fnmatch while `EXTGLOB` is associated with glob.  For users approaching this library, we will allow both so they can either use what they are familiar with, or use one for both. There will be no `NOEXTGLOB`. To the underlying library, there is no difference.  Both aliases will set the same flag.

4. Brace will always be off by default regardless of the library, so flags such as `NOBRACE` are removed in favor of `BRACE`.

Maybe a protocol multiplexer might be useful in the short term? For example, if you wanted to host HTTPS and wormhole traffic on 443. Here's one multiplexer https://github.com/yrutschle/sslh

It's basically:
* git clone ...
* cd magic-wormhole
* virtualenv ve
* source ve/bin/activate
* pip install .

The "source" line modifies your $PATH to put the virtualenv's `ve/bin` in front, so when you run `pip install .` it's using the virtualenv's `pip`, which means the `wormhole` command gets installed into `ve/bin/wormhole` (and $PYTHONPATH is set too, so it doesn't touch anything outside the virtualenv). It'll prefix a little `(ve)` to your $PROMPT to remind you which virtualenv is currently active.

You might want to `git checkout 0.10.2` before the install, to roll back to the most recent release (before we fixed a unicode problem). You might also want to use a separate virtualenv for each version of magic-wormhole that you try out (use `deactivate` to reset $PATH and $PYTHONPATH back to their original values). I frequently `rm -rf ve` the virtualenv when I'm done with them, to avoid confusion.

So aside from the change not seeming that meaningful, I have larger concerns about its correctness. For one, using `PWD` is not always accurate. If this change were to be incorporated the code should probably use the `cwd` property of the `Powerline` object that the git segment receives.

Secondly, the change won't work on Windows due to hard-coding the `/` separator. There would need to be some compatibility fixes.

Finally, I am just worried about whether this is the most accurate way to check for a git directory. For example perhaps there is a way to configure git to use a different directory name than `.git`? I don't know, personally, but I would not be surprised.

So all-in-all I lean towards not including this. Perhaps if I made a concerted effort to improve performance across the entire application it would be great. But as it stands it seems like a lot of work for minimal gains.

Ok, it looks as if somehow `response.headers` is set to `None` when the CORS extension is called.

`response.headers` should never be `None`. If no headers are passed to a response creator, sanic sets headers to `{}`, empty dict.

If you are explicitly setting `response.headers = None` somewhere in your code, that will break most extensions.

Hi!

Thanks for your message. ;) My answers below:

**Feature (1)**

> a function to report posts to admins/moderators

:+1:  This is a feature that is definitely associated with forum applications. So I agree with this proposal ; this should be implemented.

**Feature (2)**

> a banning system possibly combined with warnings users can get from moderators, so they are automatically banned for some time if they got their third warning for example

Well this is perhaps too specific to some applications. The central aim of *django-machina* is to provide a generic module that can be easily integrated into existing Django projects. This it does not make any assumptions about how a user should be considered as banned. Some projects could have a very specific policy regarding how to ban their users (this could be related to forum features... or not) and I don't want *django-machina* to interfere with that.

**Feature (3)**

> private messages, so you can talk to specific users privately

This is not directly related to forum applications. Often you will find standalone forum applications providing private messages (such as phpbb indeed). However a private messaging system is not a feature that is specific to forum applications. In the scope of a Django project you would use another third-party application to implement such a messaging system (you can see https://djangopackages.org/grids/g/messaging/ for a list of applications handling private messages between users).

**Feature (4)**

> softdelete posts so admins/moderators can still have a look at them later

:+1:  I agree, this could be necessary in some cases. So I could plan to implement it.

Can you add an issue for the features **(1)** and **(4)** separately ? :)

@jleader @EmilVarona I've pushed up some initial code for spot instances and fleets in the [issue51 branch](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/tree/issue51). You can install this in a new virtualenv with:

```
pip install git+https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker.git@issue51#egg=awslimitchecker
```

And run (for just EC2) with:

```
awslimitchecker -vv -S EC2 -u | grep spot
```

__Note__ that this requires new IAM permissions, ``ec2:DescribeSpotInstanceRequests`` and ``ec2:DescribeSpotFleetRequests``.

You should get a bunch of debug output, which includes information about each spot instance and fleet. I'll remove that before this is merged, but thought it might help with testing.

I've implemented (once again, just going by the API) the following limits:

* Max spot instance requests per region
* Max active spot fleets per region
* Max launch specifications per spot fleet
* Max target capacity per spot fleet
* Max target capacity for all spot fleets in region

I've assumed that ``Max spot instance requests per region`` only counts requests in ``open`` or ``active`` state, and that the fleet-related limits (according to my reading of the doc) only count fleets in ``active`` state (i.e. I'm totally ignoring any fleets in ``submitted`` or ``modifying`` state).

Hi,

Sorry csvimport currently only looks for ForeignKey relationships currently.
It doesnt do so in a particularly sophisticated manner, since this is CSV data.

If it finds something marked as an ForeignKey with a lookup field in its model mappings, then it checks if the data exists already for the related model and pulls back an id for the field or creates a new entry if possible in the fkey model and pulls back a new id. Based on an assumed unique lookup field.

This may be sufficient for ManyToMany fields if they are not using an intermediate model.
However the code doesn't look for these type of fields so I think it may just treat them as normal data fields.
You can try making an Fkey mapping for them and see if it works, eg. for an organisation column, org, that holds the unique name of the organisation you add
column2=org(Organisation|name)
... for it to replace with org_id FKey

The problem is that for ManyToMany then you would be getting data populated in both directions.
In general there is also a limit to how csvimport could handle this unless their was more metadata mapping options and processing of what you were storing in your csvfile in a field to look up its foreign key unique instance.

Given the possible custom behaviour required, the best option is probably just create a temporary model with the lookup data in as columns, you can use 
inspectcsv importfile.csv > models.py
to automatically generate the temporary model from your CSV file
... then use a bulk insert database query to take the data in it and populate complex relations of the final model tables appropriately.

```
    def add(self, *strings):
        """
        Add the lower case and no-period version of the string arguments to the set.

                s = s.decode(sys.stdin.encoding)

> those object_id and content_type were used to relate event or calendar to any object of your custom app. I'm not sure if removing them will break that feature.

By setting the field `content_object`, which is a GFK, Django automatically fills in the the values of `content_type` and `object_id`. Go ahead and try this in a shell:

```
>>> from schedule.models import Event
>>> from schedule.models import EventRelation
>>> from myapp.models import MyModel
>>> e = Event.objects.all()[0]
>>> m = MyModel.objects.all()[0]
>>> er = EventRelation(event=e, content_object=m)
>>> er.object_id
42424
>>> er.content_type_id
2
```

So setting the `content_object` **and** setting the `object_id`/`content_type` is redundant, just let Django take care of it. It already knows how to handle GFKs.

> The only other little annoyance is that embed-amd.js requires that ipyvolume.js be in the same folder as the embedded HTML, rather than the same folder as itself. Since I'd like to have them like this:

This is how requirejs works. It has no idea where to find the ipyvolume.js, and doesn't assume that it is the same as the embedder.

One thing you could do is declare a requirejs path somewhere that specifically tells require where to load it, using a require.config call. That config would need to happen up at the top of the page before the embed-amd include.

I've been thinking abou this and am not sure what to do...  My inclination is to try to conform to accepted norms - i.e. see what existing native and web form inputs do and try to be as close to what they do.  This way there should be least surprise to users.  More detailed thinking follows...

At least some of this is deliberate.  Using arrows within a `Layout` is supported as it is easy to decide which `Widget` is desired.  However it is less obvious when moving between `Layouts`.  For example, which button should be selected when moving down out of the check-boxes?  The first?  Or the one closest to the current `Widget`?  Or the one that was last in use?  Neither is a clear winner to me, which is why I only allow Tabbing through these boundaries.

You could well be right that most radio buttons do require explicit selection, like check boxes.  I'll compare to standard examples out there for native and web UIs and try to conform to the norm.

Multi line edit was an interesting one...  Is it better to move out of the widget when you hit the boundary or not?  I could imagine people finding it irritating that they move out of the edit box as they try to navigate to the start/end of their text.  Again, I'll have a look at what other standard UIs do before changing this.

I realized a major flaw in the code I posted. Due to cloning, identical genomes appear in the top list, thus best_genomes(n) can return n identical copies. The following fixes this by assuring each of the top n is from a different lineage. 

    def best_genomes(self, n):
        top_n = []
        used_ids = set()
        key = lambda g: g.fitness
        sorted_genomes = sorted(self.most_fit_genomes, key=key, reverse=True )
        
        i = 0
        while( i < len(sorted_genomes) and len(top_n) < n ):
            genome = sorted_genomes[i]
            if genome.ID not in used_ids:
                top_n.append(genome)
                used_ids.add(genome.ID)
            i += 1
            
        return top_n

I think the problem is sqlcontext is not handled gracefully, I'm able to overcome this problem by clearing out the locks inside the `metastore_db` folder after each test with a simple afterAll clause.

```
override def afterAll() {
    super.afterAll()
    new File("metastore_db/db.lck").delete()
    new File("metastore_db/dbex.lck").delete()
  }
```
It's not really a great workaround but that's doing the job for now.

Hey, thanks for bringing this up.

So the way it is set up currently, contexts are never actually registered with api.ai. Instead they are used only within your code to help flask-assistant match the appropriate action function for the triggered intent. 

The reason for this was to allow defining multiple action functions for the same intent, and have the correct action matched based on the contexts that are present, as shown in the docs.
However this is different than [API.AI's way](b878b7801aa2488bb869307469be7aeb), where each intent has a single defined set of required contexts and so registering contexts with intents wouldn't work correctly. Unfortunately, this causes your contexts declared in the console to be overwritten as None.

For now if you don't define the contexts in the console, but use them in your code it should work as expected, and you will still see active contexts while testing. But I think I will change the way contexts are implemented to more aligned with API.AI's method.

I'm seeing a much deeper issue with aldryn-faq+django-admin-sortable2 than just the Python attribute access problem (and I'm using version 0.6.0, which has a fix for that).

One instance of aldryn-faq's QuestionAdmin (which mixes in SortableAdminMixin) is used for both list view and detail view.  In list view, get_changelist is called and enable_sorting can be set.  In detail view, get_changelist is not called and, if list view has been loaded since server_restart, enable_sorting can still be True when SortableAdminMixin.media() is called.  It can then add list-sortable.js to the media for the response.

list-sortable.js bombs if adminsortable2's change_list.html hasn't been included, as it depends on some global variables defined in change_list.html (variables sortable_current_page and sortable_total_pages).  This in turn leads to other widgets failing to initialize.

Recap:

1. QuestionAdmin instance's enable_sorting attribute is set to True on a list view
2. still True when SortableAdminMixin.media() called on a detail view, so list-sortable.js added to media
3. when processing detail view page, browser starts running list-sortable.js code which blows up accessing global variables that are undefined (change_list.html, which defines the global variables, wasn't included)
4. browser aborts JS setup, leaving other widgets uninitialized

This is with Django 1.7.10 and Python 3.4.

As explained in the [Zinnia docs](http://docs.django-blog-zinnia.com/en/latest/how-to/extending_entry_model.html#considerations-about-the-database) with Django 1.7+ we can use the `MIGRATION_MODULES` settings to provide a full set of migrations properly inside our own projects. You can go about it like this:

You have this in your project settings already:
``` python
ZINNIA_ENTRY_BASE_MODEL = 'cmsplugin_zinnia.placeholder.EntryPlaceholder'
```
Now run `python manage.py migrate`, and afterwards `python manage.py makemigrations`.

This will create an additional migration `0003_entry_content_placeholder.py` in the migrations folder of zinnia (i.e. the zinnia package in the site-packages of your virtual environment). You can copy all migrations of Zinnia including the new one to a migrations folder in your project, e.g. `myproject/migrations/zinnia/`.

Now, add this to your project settings:
``` python
MIGRATION_MODULES = {
    'zinnia': 'myproject.migrations.zinnia',
    # (you may have other migrations here already for django CMS 3 plugins)
}
```
(Note that the Zinnia documentation suggests to collapse all prior migrations into an initial migration. However, when you do it this way your migrations fully match Zinnia's migration, which may be good for retracing changes for historical and debugging reasons one day.)

Your project migrations will have a Zinnia folder like this:
```
myproject/migrations/
├── __init__.py
└── zinnia
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── 0001_initial.py
    ├── 0002_lead_paragraph_and_image_caption.py
    └── 0003_entry_content_placeholder.py
```
Now everything is in place in your project, and you can run `python manage.py migrate`. Zinnia will work with django CMS' placeholders, and you can re-execute those migrations again from zero anytime.

Hi.

It might happen if you have concurrent jobs being executed, by default APScheduler spawns up to 10 threads.

If you google for APScheduler max_workers you will have more details.

First of all, it only happens with Python 2.7, in Python 3.4 it works fine because the logging library has different behaviour between those versions. 

Flask uses a logger called **werkzeug** with one handler **StreamHandler** attached into it that writes everything into the console. If you want you can check this out here: https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/master/werkzeug/_internal.py#L80

APScheduler uses another logger called **apscheduler.executors**, by default this logger doesn't have any handler attached for this reason you see this message **No handlers could be found for logger**.

Why does it work when calling **basicConfig()**?
Because this method attach a new **StreamHandler** to the logger called **root**, any unhandled log is handled by this root logger, it is a behaviour from logging library.

If you add manually a StreamHandler to the Root Logger it will works too.

```python
logger = logging.getLogger()  # this returns the root logger 
logger.addHandler(logging.StreamHandler())
```
Usually I fix it just calling basicConfig() in the app startup

Any question let me know.

Ripping a bit from the Ring code and your code, I was able to download all my history with this script:

```
from ring_doorbell import Ring
myring = Ring('foo@bar', 'secret')

print "Connected: "+str(myring.is_connected)
doorbell = myring.doorbells[0]

from ring_doorbell.const import (
    API_URI, DOORBELLS_ENDPOINT, DOORBELL_VOL_MIN, DOORBELL_VOL_MAX,
    DOORBELL_EXISTING_TYPE, DINGS_ENDPOINT, FILE_EXISTS,
    LIVE_STREAMING_ENDPOINT, MSG_BOOLEAN_REQUIRED, MSG_EXISTING_TYPE,
    MSG_VOL_OUTBOUND, URL_DOORBELL_HISTORY, URL_RECORDING)

history=doorbell._ring.query(API_URI + URL_DOORBELL_HISTORY.format(doorbell.account_id), extra_params={'limit':'1000'})

while(len(history) > 0):
	for event in doorbell.history():
		file=str(event['created_at'])+'_'+str(event['kind'])+'_'+str(event['answered'])+'_'+str(event['id'])
		file=file.replace(' ','_').replace(':','.')+'.mp4'
		print file
		doorbell.recording_download(event['id'],filename=file,override=False)
	# Grab more history and continue
	history=doorbell._ring.query(API_URI + URL_DOORBELL_HISTORY.format(doorbell.account_id), extra_params={'older_than':history[-1]['id']})
```

Recording some of the offline conversation: Initially the inheritance and metaclasses was for allowing code reuse of thread local code easily for multiple modules. This code was a bit complicated to understand so we moved the `threading.local` calls to module where it was needed instead of using inheritance and metaclasses for threadlocal. For adding backward compatibility, we have to make `default_ctx` and equivalents in other modules class properties. This required adding logic in base.py for the same. This included adding a metaclass and since metaclass syntax is not the same between python2 and python3 needed to use six. We cannot add six as a dependency, because that restricts users more, and thus we copied the metaclass specific code from six into base.py.

The changes introduced in https://github.com/plone/plone.api/commit/f73bddc570c05f7a1a4cea01c75b1cea6538676e breaks content creation when other fields are involved. Consider this usecase:

``` Python
from datetime import datetime

from Products.Archetypes import atapi
from Products.ATContentTypes.content import schemata
from Products.ATContentTypes.content import base

from plone import api


class IMyContent(Interface):
    """ """

MyContentSchema = schemata.ATContentTypeSchema.copy() + atapi.Schema((

    atapi.DateTimeField(
        'date',
        schemata='default',
        required=True,
        languageIndependent=True,
    ),
)


class MyContent(base.ATCTContent):
    implements(IMyContent)

    meta_type = 'MyContent'
    schema = MyContentSchema


atapi.registerType(MyContent, 'my.package')

def do_manual_creation(context):
    fields = {'title': 'A title', 'date': datetime.now()}
    content = api.content.create(context, 'MyContent', id='mycontent', **fields)

    # pre 1.6.1:
    # >>> type(content.date)
    # <type 'datetime.datetime'>

    # post 1.6.1
    # >>> type(content.date)
    # <type 'NoneType'>
```

This is because `CalendarWidget.process_form` is called with `fields` as `form`. That method does some [insane](https://github.com/plone/Products.Archetypes/blob/master/Products/Archetypes/Widget.py#L469) things, completely ignoring the passed value and finally returning `''` which gets saved by `DateTimeField` as `None`.

Pre 1.6.1, `form` was `request.form`, set by [`BaseObject._processForm`](https://github.com/plone/Products.Archetypes/blob/master/Products/Archetypes/BaseObject.py#L606), so in the abve usecase, `date` would be completely missing from the form, resulting in `empty_marker` to be returned by the widget, and the field to not be mutated; the value set by `invokeFactory` preserved.

I believe a better solution for `plone.api.content.create` would be to replace `processForm(values=kwargs)` with `processForm(values={})`. From what I can see, widgets mostly do the right thing and return `empty_marker` if `field.getName()` is missing from the request. Perfect when programatically adding content through `invokeFactory`/`plone.api.content.create` as the set values don't get modified.

I will make a pull request, with the change.

I'll chime in to say that I'm experiencing the same issue on PyCharm 5.0.4 under Python 3.4.4 64bit installed using conda.  My pymq version is 15.2.0.  If I load it into IPython the autocompletion works just fine and `"DEALER" in dir(zmq)` returns `True` in IPython and the default python REPL.  The constants are certainly in `__all__` for `zmq`, `zmq.sugar`, and ultimately `zmq.backend.cython.constants`.  I'll say that `zmq.constants.<TAB>` does not show the constants but instead just the values like `base_names`, `int_sockopt_names`, etc.  

PyCharm gives the following warning when it can't find a name, such as `zmq.DEALER`:

> This inspection detects names that should resolve but don't. Due to dynamic dispatch and duck typing, this is possible in a limited but useful number of cases. Top-level and class-level items are supported better than instance items.

So I think what is happening is that PyCharm is not actually importing `zmq` to provide completions but instead relying on static analysis which is failing in this particular case.  To get it to play well with PyCharm you would probably have to list each export individually.

Hope is not lost, Python has a trick up it's sleeve.  I noticed while poking around in the pyzmq code that you've used the style

```python
from module import submodule1
from module.submodule1 import *
from module import submodule2
from module.submodule2 import *

__all__ = submodule1.__all__ + submodule2.__all__
```
But Python has a better way through `from __future__ import absolute_import`
```python
from __future__ import absolute_import

from module.submodule1 import *
from module.submodule2 import *

__all__ = submodule1.__all__ + submodule2.__all__
```

So if you have them listed out explicitly in `__all__` in some lower level module you can use this style all the way up in a less dynamic manner.  This also works in Python 2, I think as early as 2.5 with the `__future__` import.  I don't know how well it plays with Cython imports, though.

Here's some docs on what SO_REUSEPORT is for and what it does:
https://lwn.net/Articles/542629/

It's basically so you can have multiple servers share the same port. This
doesn't make sense for a server that you're using as a central lock to
schedule processes.

The current hack is that in different execution environments (different global parameters), the one and same instantiated task (some RangeBase-child) requires *different* tasks for the same set of parameters it is input with. It's like using global variables. If we explicitly thread along all parameters from RangeBase, it becomes more like a pure and global-state-independent task.

            except:                                                                
                pass   
        raise futureKilled

Hi @kislyuk sorry about the long wait for a reply. We needed a long discussion on how we would like to proceed about this.

First of all, this is something that we definitely want because it would improve the experience in coding with botocore/boto3. Catching a general ClientError and introspecting the error code is cumbersome. So thank you for starting the conversation. Here is some feedback that we have on this:

* We think that the errors should be namespaced to services because services often have overlapping exceptions which could cause confusion if you accidentally caught an exception from a different service that you did not intend for.
* It would be great if exceptions could be scoped to sessions. Right now client classes across sessions are different, that is ``session1.client('s3'), session2.client('s3'``) produce different classes. Putting classes in a module level (global) errorfactory means there's no granularity across sessions which is inconsistent from the rest of botocore.
* How would you propose handling API versions? Across the various API versions, the types exceptions available may differ.
* It would be nice if we could warn when you are importing an exception that does not exist in the model and possibly be able to enumerate/document them. This would really help with discoverability as right now there are two ways (neither of them ideal) you can determine the valid exceptions: look at the model or try calling the operation and running into exception yourself)

Another idea that we have had offline discussions about is expose exceptions as such:
```py
try:
    ec2.describe_instances(...)
except ec2.meta.exceptions.InvalidInstanceId as e:
    pass
```
Dangling it off the ``meta`` property is nice for a few reasons:
* It is scoped to the client/session
* It handles API versions
* It is namespaced based on service name
* It would not be difficult to use the service model to enumerate exceptions.

The main downside is that you would have to reference to the client/resource to be able to catch the exception. Let us know what you think about that idea?

I realize that this is a lot of feedback, but I think having a good discussion about this is important as getting the functionality working in a way that covers all of the edge cases and has an easy to use interface is very important for this feature.
I will do it shortly after. Thanks!

Feel free to suggest a solution, however, to me it seems the main culprit is that GitPython uses its own repository in read-only mode at all. This is bad and shouldn't be done, but also speeds up tests considerably.

Thus, to me this issue is "won't fix", but I am open to suggestions if they don't involve a massive amount of work coming my way :).

What kind of problem do you have with colors for regression? If you don't know how to construct to color:
first you need to create the palette with something like

    palette = ContinuousPaletteGenerator(*variable.colors)

where `variable` is the continuous variable that defines the coloring. The color is then

    palette.getRGB(value)

where `value` is the value of the `variable`. `values` can also be a numpy array, if that's more convenient.

I had this issue today with a fresh pip install of djangocms-installer.  I traced the problem to the "tzlocal" package, which attempts to determine the local timezone.  The tzlocal.get_localzone() function for *nix systems goes through a series of checks to try to find the timezone setting.  For my system, which is running Fedora 20, the method it fell back to was to look at the path of "/etc/localtime", which is apparently usually a symlink on systemd-based systems.  On my system it was not a symlink, but rather a copy of the zoneinfo file from /usr/share/zoneinfo, so it could not find a timezone from the path data and was therefore falling back to a timezone called "local", which gets stuffed into the "settings.py" file that djangocms creates.  django's manage.py then pukes on the "local" setting of the timezone since it does not exist in /usr/share/zoneinfo.  So, if you're having problems with the timezone, check the search order out for tzlocal.get_localzone() function out for your platform.  I fixed the problem for my platform by replacing the /etc/localtime file with a symlink to the correct file in /usr/share/zoneinfo, but the problem seems like it could also be fixed temporarily by setting the "TZ" env variable to the correct timezone.

This feels to me like it is adding another quirk to python packaging. If we do this, `setup.py` will depend on `Pipfile.lock` which is *very* weird: now abstract dependencies in `setup.py` will be generated from a file supposed to contain concrete dependencies (`Pipfile.lock`).

As the use case if for libraries, doesn't this also mean that now libraries have to publish their `Pipfile.lock` so that `setup.py` works? But that's against the general principle that libraries are not supposed to publish their lockfile as they need to be able to work with many different versions of their dependencies.

To me, a much better way to handle this would be to extend the Pipfile with enough information to automatically *generate* a `setup.py` from it. But maybe I am not understanding the distinction between `setup.py` and `Pipfile` well enough? Is there a case where you'd want to have *both* a `Pipfile` and a `setup.py` with different information? In all cases I can imagine, these two files would contain the same information (with `setup.py` having additional meta data, and `Pipfile` specifying additional deps for other environments): abstract dependencies that my application/library depends on to run ((1) and (2) in the classification above).

Hi all,

If you look at the code for `threshold_niblack` and `threshold_sauvola`, you'll see that the window-size parameter accepts only an integer. The idea here would be to accept a tuple with the same number of elements as the input image has dimensions. So, for example, `window_size=(3, 5)` would create a window of 3 rows by 5 columns. `window_size=(3, 15, 15)` would create a 3D window size of 3 planes by 15 rows by 15 columns. (Please refer to our documentation on [coordinate conventions](http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/numpy_images.html#coordinate-conventions), too.

This is the same way that Gaussian filter accepts both a single sigma as well as a tuple sigma.

I hope this helps!

So I was thinking about this deprecation problem and got prettty far into making a deprecation decoration:

commit showing a few examples:
https://github.com/hmaarrfk/scikit-image/commit/bde84f9012cba5fb347436abc06de7005d367489
Main file:
https://github.com/hmaarrfk/scikit-image/blob/deprecations/skimage/_shared/deprecations.py

It uses a decorator, introspection, and docstring rewriting to actually detect if the user passed in the argument. If they did, then it doesn't issue any warnings. If they didn't it issues a `FutureWarning`.

It also writes the docstring message and the deprecation warning for you and points the user to their line of code that they should fix.

```
    If the software version is greater or equal to that of ``version``, this
    decorator returns the original function without any modifications.
    It will warn the user, pointing to the line of his code, in a single
    warning for all changes in the default parameters.
    This decorator does the following:
        - Adds a human readable message whenever the function is invoked
          without specifying the deprecated keyword argument.
        - It modifies the ``__signature__`` of the function so that signature
          reflects the current default parameters.
        - Modifies the docstring so as to include a warning about the
          deprecation information compatible numpydoc.
```

I was going to put this in a little BSD package and show it to `scikit-image` and ask for help developing features.

One other advantage of this kind of decorator is that the git commit history would be cleaned up quite a bit. The decorators can even be left in, and if they are, they only slow down loading time, and not execution time.

We can play around with other decorators and `kwargs` introspection to basically get whatever behaviour we want (man python can do crazy things).

A few other deprecation decorators I can think of:
  * args -> kw only deprecation
  * parameter renaming (as mentionned by @soupault)
  * coordinate swapping ;) @jni

If you want a portion of the Mapping to be optional you need to add both `default=colander.drop` and `missing=colander.drop` or it will raise an exception.  If you skip the serialization step and just do a recursive str() then that simplifies the schema quite a bit.  We'd then just drop looking at 'default' and use just 'missing'.  Unless someone sees a need to keep 'default' and preparers then this is probably a better way to go.

@tiborsimko concerning cataloguers to use UUID:

1. I think they will accept them easily
2. but they should not be 40 digits hex codes like git commit ids (IMHO this is not necessary in this usecase)

A real world example where cataloguers handle UUIDs in their native cataloguing interface (`bibedit` in inveniospeak) would e.g. be GBV (the largest union catalogue in Germany; same is true for all other PICA installations) where this is in production for [place a long time her].

What's done, however, is that the editor offers a simple lookup interface to retrieve the UUID easily as outlined above. Also their UUIDs look more like this: 81250898X. IOW: just don't make them to wild ;-) Having source of ids in some other field to make them exchangeable is usually not a problem as well. (`0247_ $2 $a` thingies in [Marc](http://www.loc.gov/marc/bibliographic/bd024.html).) That allows even for quite "lenghty" UUIDs as the final id is formed like `strcat($2, $a)`. In Chris paper guys like [`author:(DE-588b)141792906`](http://d-nb.info/gnd/141792906) ar of this form.

With UUIDs like this cataloguers to my experience are even happy exchange them phone. It was common practice at my job that I got a number like this to refer to a dataset. Note also that their `bibedit` takes care of filling in some fields automagically once the UUID is in place. This helps consistency.

There is a philosophical problem with tuples as they are immutable. You can not really apply a patch on them as it would mean creating new object. Please convert your tuples to lists.

```ipython
In [1]: from dictdiffer import *

In [2]: d1 = {'a': 10.0}

In [3]: d2 = {'a': 10.5}

In [4]: d3 = {'a': [10.0,11,12]}

In [5]: d4 = {'a': [10.5,11,12]}

In [6]: result1 = diff(d1, d2, tolerance=0.1)

In [7]: result2 = diff(d3, d4, tolerance=0.1)

In [8]: print "result1: ", list(result1)
result1:  []
print "result2: ", list(result2)
In [9]: print "result2: ", list(result2)
result2:  []
```
IMHO `dictdiffer` should take care only about "diffing" of objects. You can create new package for data conversion or keep the `convert_to_numerical` function in your code.

Some considerations on this topic: 

- in Invenio 1.x legacy, we have indeed one PO message catalogue that is composed of Python output message and HTML/WML template output messages.  Such a combined catalogue has clear advantages for the translator, since a message appears there exactly once, wherever it is used.  However, in theory one could also have two catalogues, one for the frontend and one for the backend, and automatise message translation propagations via `msgmerge`.

- in Invenio 3, where we have 100+ repositories, there is an additional complication in that some phrase such as "Yes" and "No" could be used all over in various places.  So the `msgmerge` scripting magic would be required in an inter-repository manner in order to ease the translation job for people.

- What would selecting `angular-gettext` mean for non-Angular JS files?  We still have some around... Adopting Angular-only solution would mean to spread Angular more widely into various Invenio packages, possibly opening the question about going for one single Angular app.  So this is another thing to consider...

Hi @ccordoba12 ,

> packages that only support PyQt4/5 and still wanted to use a qtpy converted package, because it kind of defeats the purpose of qtpy

That's funny because precisely I saw the biggest value in qtpy for *precisely that* situation:

For example, spyder provides widgets that may be used in another library (e.g., [taurus](https://github.com/taurus-org/taurus)). But that library (or the programs which use that library) are not ready to move to PyQt5... then (as I saw it) thanks to qtpy, spyder can move on and support PyQt5 while still being useful to those who are tied to PyQt4.

In fact if all the projects in the dependency chain could all move to PyQt5 at the same time, I see little point in qtpy: I would switch to PyQt5 directly (yes, I would lose PySide compatibility, but given the lack of development in PySide, that is not something I would worry about)

> May I ask why is not possible for you to use qtpy to convert your codebase, as several other projects have done?

In fact converting to PyQt5 is high on our TODO list, and we consider using qtpy for the above stated reason but still the people using our library (taurus) have a lot of code which cannot be migrated soon and/or in a coordinated way. And we must support them until they are ready to migrate.

If we simply migrated taurus to qtpy (with qtpy raising exceptions as it is now), we would be pushing the problem towards our users.

Thank you, Charles, for such a comprehensive explanation.

Let me answer each point in turn:

> Replace Vincenty with my algorithms. [...] In this scheme, the routines with "vincenty" in their names would be deprecated and would merely call the corresponding routines with "vincenty" replaced by "geodesic".

Vincenty deprecation sounds reasonable to me, but I would rather not implicitly substitute `vincency` implementation with `geodesic`, because some users might rely on vincenty's inaccuracy or the 4-7x faster computation speed. So I would prefer to still keep vincenty, but issue a warning for its usages with an advice to use a generic `distance` class instead, which provides an up-to-date method of distance computation (`geodesic`, for the moment).

In this case removing vincenty example and explanation from the docs is fine with me, but `vincenty` class should be marked as deprecated with:
- a `warning` in `measure` and `destination` methods with [`DeprecationWarning` category](https://docs.python.org/2/library/warnings.html#warning-categories) and an advice to use `geodesic` instead
- a `.. warning::` block in `vincenty` doc, explaining why `vincenty` is deprecated (because it's less accurate and in some cases fails to converge) and what to use instead (`distance` class).

> Either make geographiclib a dependency of geopy or just include the code as part of geopy.

I'm fine with adding a dependency on `geographiclib`. I would say vendoring is not needed here (unless there's an issue with any of the geopy's supported Python versions which cannot be fixed on your (`geographiclib`) side, but this looks unlikely).

> This would also allow immediate access to routines for computing the area of geodesic polygons and for computing differential properties of geodesics (e.g., how much does the end point of a geodesic point move when the initial azimuth is changed by a small amount) which are useful, e.g., in doing adjustments of triangulation networks.

I would expect that users who actually need that kind of advanced measurements are aware of `geographiclib` itself, and I tend to think that the higher level geopy is probably not for them. Thereby, I'd rather keep the interfaces as simple as they're now, without bringing in peculiar features which aren't needed by most users.

> Various options are available if speed really is an issue: [...]. However, I regard this as secondary to getting decent algorithms into the hands of your users.

I agree with the last point, whereas I believe that speed is *really* an issue for some of the users, so having an easy way (like installing pyproj) to quickly enhance performance without tinkering with binary extensions on the user side would be great. If we tend to think of pyproj as a solution to this, then we may simply add an [extras to `setup.py`](https://github.com/geopy/geopy/blob/67a18801cc87eec40e1f1e8edaa6f297228be91e/setup.py#L32), which would allow to bring in pyproj with simply doing something like `pip install geopy[fastgeodesic]`.

> * hook up to pyproj
> * provide a numpy implementation
> * use Cython to call the C++ library

I would rather not introduce any required binary extension (or required dependency with one, like numpy or pyproj) which might make installation more complicated even if a user is going to use only the geocoders part of the library, so building a native extension is kinda useless for them. Also compiling CPython extensions for pypy (which geopy supports at the moment) might be tricky. Thus I believe that a python-only solution is a requirement here.

To sum up, here are my suggestions:
- vincenty should be deprecated, but not substituted with `geodesic`
- a python-only solution for `geodesic` should be used by default to avoid platform-specific issues
- having a way to optionally install a binary dependency to speed up computations would be great (but I'm totally OK to move this out of the scope of this PR to simplify things for now).

I've been thinking today a bit about the API for using the second kick.  It would be nice if this was completely transparent, i.e., if the second kick automatically got applied if the user was drawing through a phase screen using photon-shooting, and automatically didn't get applied if the user was drawing using DFT.  The problem I'm facing is that these two situations not only require second kick or no second kick, they also require generating phase screens with our without the high-k turbulence included.  I could delay constructing the screens I suppose, but even in that case one wouldn't be able to easily switch back and forth between DFT and photon-shooting using the same atmosphere object.

So I think the solution may be to just require the user to explicitly create a screen with or without high-k turbulence, attempt to infer from the screen whether or not a second kick should be added to the PSF (although, allow the user to override this inference), and then issue a (suppressible) warning if the user then tries to draw using the inappropriate method.  I.e., warn if the user tries to draw a second-kick-included PSF using DFT, or a second-kick-excluded PSF using photon-shooting.  Any thoughts?
