I was playing around with Golang and writing Terraform Providers, and wrote an Ansible Tower Terraform Provider, which solves this use case (not that it is actually helpful to you).

This allows you to flexibly write down and manage via GIT the entire "state" of a Tower configuration, including how each type relates to the other. Cool things like doing massive changes to jobs/templates/orgs/etc become simple, (via Terraform Modules, for example), moving jobs between groups/user, etc, just handled via the TF configuration. Moving a complex Tower configuration between Tower environments in a controlled way then becomes trivial.

This issue could then be solved by updating the IP address of the Tower API, and re-running Terraform on the host.

There's no documentation, there are several showstopper bugs, but it works, at least for the example.

[Example Config](https://github.com/mpeter/terraform-provider-tower/blob/master/examples/main.tf)
[Terraform Tower Provider](https://github.com/mpeter/terraform-provider-tower/tree/master/tower)
[Go Tower Client API](https://github.com/mpeter/go-towerapi/tree/master/towerapi)

So I took a look at this, and it's basically a result of the DyNet memory allocator not having any concept of garbage collection. When a parameter is allocated the amount of memory the memory allocator is using is incremented, but there's no way to free memory (other than freeing *all* the memory, which would kill all of the parameters that we have loaded).

I thought about this a bit, and I think it might be useful to generalize the DyNet memory management so that we can support different types of allocators:
* A "normal" memory allocator that uses standard OS function calls to allocate and free memory. This could be used for things that don't need to be super-fast.
* The super-fast memory allocator that we're using now.
* Some other kind of smart memory allocator that falls between the two.

This may be another eye of the beholder. Prior to this change part of the knowledge of protbuf paths is in `generate-grpc.sh` and part in `build.rs` to trigger changed re-runs. In addition `build.rs` has no easy way to get `CACHE_ROOT` right when emitting changed lines for `grpc_rust_plugin` and `protoc-gen-rust`. Finally that mechanism required cargo-ensure-installed.

Here, as much of the logic as possible is self-contained with only the binary download of protoc shelled out. Its true the logic is still spread between multiple bits. Prior to this change, it was `build.rs`, `generate-grpc.sh`, `download_binary.sh` and `bootstrap.sh`, now it's `build.rs`, `download_binary.sh` and `bootstrap_rust.sh`. Things seem slightly simpler to follow to me anyway.

Yep, there is a way to do it. To update a remote file, you need to create with `drive.CreateFile({'id': '<your file id>'})` and then perform the action you would like to perform, in your case `SetContentFile()` and `Upload()`.

If you need to get the ID of the file programmatically, you can either list all files or [list files in a specific folder](https://github.com/googledrive/PyDrive/issues/37) and search for the file by name in the list (how to [upload a file to a folder](https://github.com/googledrive/PyDrive/blob/master/examples/using_folders.py) may also come in handy).

A better way to do this, however, would be to use query parameters as described [here](https://developers.google.com/drive/v2/web/search-parameters) when running the `ListFiles()` command, i.e.:
```python
file_list = drive.ListFile({'q': "<your query here>"}).GetList()
```

Coming back to your original question - how to deal with columns with multiple categorical features - then the temporary workaround would be to employ the following two-stage workflow:
1. Take the original dataset, and "explode" single collection-type columns to multiple scalar-type columns (eg. using the `MultiLabelBinarizer` transformation). Do not do any other feature engineering in this step.
2. Take the "exploded" dataset, and work with it as usual (feature transformation, estimation).

SkLearn2PMML/JPMML-SkLearn is currently able to handle the second stage. You would need to maintain a separate Python/Java solution for handling the first stage.

Hi @twosky2000,
looking at the soco.discover it looks like once it finds one zone player it uses that player to give details of all (every player knows about the other players - in that household!)
This was done to speed it up rather than wait for discovery to return each device.
Therefore whichever player is discovered first it will show all players in that household group.

With current code I don't see how you can choose which household to discover. I believe an option could be added to discover to specify the household_id you want and then discover would wait for the first discovery with that household_id. This would be pretty easy to implement.

Is that what you are looking for?
Do others think this approach would work and makes sense?

Cheers David

# Deprecate now, maintain until users that report in are done porting (or Python end-of-life at the latest) except for new functionality

* deprecate support for 2.7 with the next release
* collect and keep track of porting issues of users and plan to remove support entirely when they are done, or when 2.7 reaches official end-of-life in 2020
* until then, continue to fix any bugs and maintain existing code and features
* for new features, if additional effort for achieving 2.7-comatability would be too high, support 3.x only (under the condition that no other features are broken)

This is something we must look into it carefully. Some web browsers are more forgiving in a way that they might detect a compressed file and decompress it even if the server did not tell it specifically. A few things to be considered when making an action plan.

* While we do read the original `Content-Type` from the archived headers to replay, we currently do not look into the `Content-Encoding` header, which we should.
* There are some well known and wide-spread violations of recommendations about the `Content-Encoding` and `Transfer-Encoding`, so we need to be careful about those.
* We considered some permutations of content encoding, compression, and encryption (whether or not the original content was encoded/compressed) in our research publication and have more experiments planned in this direction.

@dfhkvs I'll update the docs to reflect this, but a much better option is to `$ pip install planet --user`.  This installs the package within a subdirectory in your home directory and avoids a bunch of potential collisions and frustrations.  The one downside is that executables are placed in a directory that is probably not already on your path.

Packages are installed here:

```
~/Library/Python/$VERSION/lib/python/site-packages/
```

and executables are installed here:

```
~/Library/Python/$VERSION/bin/
```

It looks like you're on Python 2.7 so replace `$VERSION` with `2.7`.  If that directory is not already on your `$PATH`, add this line to your `~/.bash_profile`:

```
export PATH="$HOME/Library/Python/2.7/bin:$PATH"
```

This does the same and will handle Python upgrades and changes in username:

```
PY_USER_BIN=$(python -c "import os, site; print(os.path.join(site.getuserbase(), 'bin'))")
export PATH="${PY_USER_BIN}:${PATH}"
```

### Why is this better? ###

Using `$ pip install --user` is superior in a number of ways:

1. **The most important reason**: `$ sudo pip install` _can_ execute arbitrary code as root.  If a package cannot be installed as a [wheel](https://pythonwheels.com) the installation process executes `setup.py` directly, which means that `$ sudo pip install` is the same as `$ sudo python setup.py` for source distributions.  Incidentally, we neglected to upload a Python 2 wheel for the most recent release of this package, so a `$ sudo pip install planet` at the very least triggers this package's `setup.py` to be executed as root.
2. Using `$ sudo pip install` installs the package globally and can introduce really heinous conflicts between different versions of `pip`, `python`, and other users.
3. Installing packages in individual virtual environment is a great method for isolating specific workflows, working around conflicting dependencies, or developing new code.  In this case it probably makes the `$ planet` CLI a bit harder to use.

### Cool.  What do I do now? ###

I think your virtual environment install is incorrect because you installed `planet` within the virtual environment as `sudo`.  Unless you have a specific reason for isolating the package within a virtual environment I would recommend uninstalling all possible copies of `planet`, deleting the virtual environment you created, and then installing with the `--user` flag.  Here are the steps:

1. Re-activate the virtual environment: `$ source env/bin/activate`
2. Uninstall the `sudo` installed version of `planet`: `$ sudo pip uninstall planet`
3. Make sure no other versions of `planet` are installed in the environment: `$ pip uninstall planet`
4. Deactivate the environment: `$ deactivate`
5. Delete the environment.
6. Uninstall any lingering global `sudo` installed versions of `planet`: `$ sudo pip uninstall planet`
7. Uninstall any other lingering `planet` installations: `$ pip uninstall planet`
8. Install `planet` for your user only: `$ pip install planet --user`

If for some reason you do want to keep `planet` isolated within its own virtual environment, perform steps 1 - 5 and then:

6. Create a new environment: `$ python -m virtualenv env`
7. Activate the environment: `$ source env/bin/activate`
8. Ensure the packaging related tools contained in the environment are up to date: `$ pip install pip setuptools wheel --upgrade`
9. Install `planet` within the environment: `$ pip install planet`

Hi @rfhigler, thanks for your issue report.

After `locate` performs the bandpass, it always normalizes the image and coerces it to integer type. Which bitsize it uses, depends on the input image: if the original image was of float type, it will be an uint8; if the original image was of integer type, it will take that datatype (for you: uint16). That is what line 567 is about. If you do the preprocessing yourself, trackpy does not know which original dtype it was, and it will always normalize to uint8. This could give minor differences, but not as large as you describe.

A second issue is the intensity-based feature filtering (parameter `minmass`). This parameter should be change accordingly with your input image intensity: for float images this would be much lower than for integer images. There is always a default value set: for integer values, it is 100, for float values it is 1. I think this is what you should tune.

If you turn off intensity-based filtering (`minmass=0`), you could in principle reproduce your results if you do the normalizing the same as `locate` does (use `trackpy.preprocessing.scale_to_gamut`). If you use mass-based filtering, there is no way to exactly reproduce the result with custom preprocessing, because `minmass` is defined differently.

@danielballan I am :+1: on changing the current default behaviour of minmass, as it really obscures these kind of things. Also we should really take a look to issue #257, maybe we can put the rescaling in `bandpass` or in `local_maxima`.

The string substitution strategy would work, but making deisctl know it has to perform that substitution isn't any better than making it know it needs to drop the @# before execing into the container.  The difference between the two, however, is that changing how the containers are named is a more sweeping change that we should try to avoid as long as the simpler alternative that's narrower in scope exists.

> logspout and publisher, for example, which are also distributed components, don't seem to get an ordinal representation

There's a reason for that.  Those are global units.  They simply run one and only one everywhere that fleet metadata permits them to.  Contrast that with something like router where we are in _control_ of how many we scale to.  Not simply requiring one and only one calls for a different approach in ensuring no two of such a component ever run on a single host.

Also, as you've observed, the global nature of units like logspout or publisher makes the idea of `deisctl dock logspout@1` impossible.  There's no way in fleet to enumerate global units.  I wouldn't worry about this.  `deisctl dock` is a convenience feature in the first place.  It's still possible to get into any container by ssh'ing to the right machine and then using `nse` or `docker exec`.

> Also, any existing code in deisctl that references router container names by their current, common shorthand name (e.g., just router) will need to be updated as well. So, perhaps this isn't an easy one.

I know first hand that the code used for resolving component names is a little complex.  I don't believe, however, that it needs to be touched at all for this.

The key is remembering what `deisctl dock <component>` actually does.  It's resolving the component name (which is a little complex), figuring out what host that component lives on, ssh'ing to there, then doing a `docker exec <container name>` on the host.  It's _only_ the `<container name>` in this last part that needs to change.  We probably don't need more than a couple line of code to do this.  Something like the following pseudocode:

```
declare containerName
if componentName includes "@":
  containerName = componentName.substring(0, componentName.indexOf("@")
else:
  containerName = componentName
```

If etcd punts out a member due to a failed heartbeat, the data may still be there, and we wouldn't want to potentially lose that data - particularly in the case where _many_ nodes disappear entirely when an AWS Availability Zone becomes partitioned.

Remember the 'P' in CAP? That's really what we're talking about here.

It's one thing to lose etcd membership temporarily. Re-syncing the data from etcd is minor compared to gigabytes of data stored in ceph.

If AWS has TERMINATED an instance, there is no way to get the data back. At that point, we would want he auto-scaling group to send a notification to the deis cluster, which can be routed to something with the ability to prune the terminated instance from ceph to retain its quorum.

This is less to do with having the ceph-osds lay around and pile up than it is to do with the ceph-mon quorum being retained.

Hi @AlessandroChecco  We found the issue: this was hitting an edge case in one of our algorithms for highly non-graph-like datasets. Most of the data in that workload was points with no connections, or edges with no adjacent edges, which an otherwise fast approximate community detection algorithm was not expecting.

> I've been considering adding a final check so it tries to decode the string with the predicted encoding and eliminates any that are invalid, but I have not gotten a chance to add that yet.

I'd rather we didn't. Here are a few reasons why:

1. str.encode/str.decode can trigger imports. Delayed imports in a web application can cause deadlocks in acquiring the import lock on certain versions of Python. Requests already has had this problem and I'd rather not add it to chardet.

2. I believe we can return all of our most confident detections. We can provide an extra function to users to do this for them, but I don't think we should do it as part of `detect`.

Yes, GGD stands for Go Get Data. 

We utilize the extra key to store information about species, genome build, key words, tags, etc., which fundamentally describe the unique differences between packages. Because GGD is being developed to reach a wide range of fields, beyond the genomic and biology fields, these unique identifiers in the extra key will be propagated with data field unique information. (Physics, pharmaceutical, financial, the -omics fields, etc). This makes it difficult to make this information more formal.

Your are ok. If we had to just forward clean it might work. But we cannot test that, because paths need to be rewritten. Indeed, the objectivo of putting an intermediate wrapper is to modify something. As soon as `echo $@ | sed` or `for a in $@` are used, spaces are interpreted as breaks.

Then, as you say, a different language must be used. Writting an additional program in C or Golang is of little help, because it should be compiled for each platform. Then, since VUnit is written in Python, the most sensible approach is to do it in VUnit:

```
if os.environ.get('VUNIT_SIMULATOR_DOCKER_IMG') is not None:
   for i in range(len(cmd)):
      cmd[i] = shlex.quote(cmd[i])
```

At this point, it makes sense to do string replacements in the same place:

```
if os.environ.get('VUNIT_SIMULATOR_DOCKER_IMG') is not None:
   vunit_dir=os.environ.get('VUNIT_DIR')
   for i in range(len(cmd)):
      cmd[i] = shlex.quote(cmd[i].replace(vunit_dir,'/vunit'))
```

Moreover, because VUNIT_DIR and PROJECT_DIR are already available in VUnit, I'm guessing how to reuse them, instead of reading the envvars. Indeed, if the replacement is done inside VUnit (which was my very first proposal in this thread), there is no need for escaping: "$@" will work.

`aws_sign_request: True

The credentials required to sign the requests will then be retrieved from the environment by checking these locations:
- Environment variables

You can extend the save method on DocType as follows:

```python
def save(self, ** kwargs):
        self.lines = len(self.body.split())
        self['@timestamp'] = datetime.now()
        return super(Article, self).save(** kwargs)
```

If you send to an existing logstash index, you may need to explicitly set the timestamp format, for example:

```python
def save(self, ** kwargs):
        self.lines = len(self.body.split())
        t = datetime.now()
        self['@timestamp'] = t.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%fZ')
        return super(Article, self).save(** kwargs)
```

More information [here](https://www.freshlex.com/add-timestamp-to-your-python-elasticsearch-dsl-model.html).

There are a few blog posts covering this which I hope will help, say [this one]. (https://blog.jhnr.ch/2018/05/16/working-with-azure-cli-behind-ssl-intercepting-proxy-server/). To configure proxy with user name and password, check out [here](https://askubuntu.com/questions/82880/how-do-i-set-a-system-wide-proxy-with-a-username-and-password)

Can only reproduce this issue on a Windows machine, with cmd.exe. For long-running operations, we use a third-party library called humanfriendly to indicate the progress and animate the spinner. Once the LRO is finished, the third-party library writes an ANSI escape sequence (called `erase_line_code` in the third-party library) `'\r\x1b[K'` to the standard out stream to remove the spinner. Also noticed that [the erase_line_code was actually modified to an empty string](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blame/dev/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/commands/progress.py#L13) for Windows machines. That means that we are writing an empty string to the standard out stream when the LRO is finished, causing the spinner not to disappear for Windows machines.

Semantic indents can't be (trivially) fixed with a linter.

If you went to a person and said, "Hey this tool automatically indents your Python code" would they think it means changing semantic indents?

Let's assume that pre-commit success will explode and it will be inundated with a huge number of hooks available. As each test is usually inexpensive people will be likely to want to add more to their projects, like 20-30 or even more. If each of this would point to another git repository installation would become a real issue.

I am in favour of having hooks defined in a centralized repository (registry?) (without loosing ability to have additional repos) but at the same time I do not expect this repository to be used for development of these hook. Hook dependencies should be installed per hook instead of per repository.

One way or another I think you will struggle with "simple", it will grow regardless your effort to keep the goal small.  I think that the trick here is to decouple development and maintenance of hooks from their publication in a centralised registry.

There is also one thing that concerns me a lot, security: without a central "trustable" registry it would be too easy to add some cool hook today which could inject some nasty stuff into thousands of project two years from now. I am not saying that someone will do it by design, but once you have 10 git repositories pointing to 10 different individuals on github, you are creating an easy way for someone to hack you build process: they need to hack only one of these accounts. This is one place where package repositories are doing a little bit better job. For example if something bad happens on pypi, i can contact them by email or irc report the malicious item and in (hopefully) minutes it will be taken down. I doubt this will happen so quick with github repositories or other git servers.

One thing for sure, future is going to be interesting ;)

Two answer your questions:

> what are the two collision shape types involved

A cylinder and a convex hull shape

> which algorithm is used to compute the closest (contact) points (GJK, EPA, SAT etc)

This particular query is for colliding objects, so I assume EPA. (ultimately invoked by a call to `btCollisionWorld::performDiscreteCollisionDetection`).

> Is there any contact caching (persistent contact manifold) enabled (which it is by default)?
  
Technically, yes, but the result I'm looking at is the very first invocation of the collision detection, so persistence shouldn't be an issue. 

> Are the closest points (penetration depth) location unique (sphere vs sphere) or not (two polyhedra with parallel touching faces)? 

This is *possible* but highly unlikely.  I haven't delved specifically into the exact scenario but the odds that the contact is on the cap of the cylinder that is perfectly aligned with the colliding face is so small, I assume not.

> It helps to use the double precision version of Bullet if you care about collision quality, are you using double or single precision?

Yes, double.

`json_response` function provides `dumps` parameter that allow you to specify

So add `json` parameter to `web.Response` would require to add another parameter `json_dumps` or whatever.

I was suggesting uninstalling and installing `jupyter_core`, not `jupyter`.

The `jupyter_core` package provides the `jupyter command. All of the other Jupyter packages depend on this.

The `jupyter` package contains no code, but it depends on all of the jupyter pieces, so it's a quick way for people to install everything. However, I don't think uninstalling it with pip will affect anything else, so it probably won't fix a broken installation.

I am not sure what the fix is, but I see roughly where the problem is:

* In `tornado>=4` there is a `WebSocketHandler.check_origin(self, origin)` method that checks the origin of web sockets. It has a required positional argument.
* In jupyter/notebook, we have a subclass that we use in our handlers `ZMQStreamHandler(WebSocketMixin, WebSocketHandler)` which also has the `check_origin` method with the required positional.
* In terminado there is `TermSocket(tornado.websocket.WebSocketHandler)` which has a `check_origin` method with an *optional* `keyword argument`.
* In `TermSocket.open` `self.check_origin()` is called with no arguments.
* At first glance this looks like it should work fine because it will be calling terminado's subclasses `check_origin`.
* *But*, the code which calls open in juipyter/notebook does so by doing `super(WebSocketMixin, self).open(*args, **kwargs)`.
* Somehow, that is causing the base tornado `check_origin` to be called, but with *no arguments*, which results in an exception.

The part that I am still confused about is that `WebSocketMixin` doesn't have the base tornado `WebSocketHandler` in its mro, so I don't see how it is resolving to that.

Questions:

* All of this logic in terminado is to handle tornado<4. But tornado 3 is really old now and jupyter/notebook now requires tornado>=4. Can we just remove all this logic from terminado?
* Is there something more subtle going on with the inheritance here that needs to be fixed?
* The tornado `check_origin` method has never had a different signature. Buy terminado subclassing and changing the signature, this was bound to happen at some point. If we keep the logic in terminado,  can we address this API difference?

I don't agree with putting this login inside schematics. I believe there is a simple way of doing this using regular python and it is already done elsewhere in the codebase.

The basic idea is that your `__init__` method looks for specific keys in `**kwargs`. If it finds them, you can get it with `kwargs.pop('key')` and also remove it from the list of things passed to `super(...)`.

If a keyword argument you didn't expect is passed in, `super(...)` will be the one that notices it and throws an error.

But isn't this almost exactly how it already works? It's just that `export` is currently named either `to_primitive` or `export_loop` depending on whether it's a basic or compound type. If you're talking about this:

```python
if hasattr(field, 'export_loop'):
  ...
else:
  ...
```

then I agree this is totally crazy and precisely what got me started on #266.

If you're talking about why there is an external `export_loop` in the `transforms` module, then that's because the main loops are general import/export processors that can act on arbitrary mappings as well as actual `Model` instances.

For example, `validate()` invokes `import_loop` to run a model's validators on a dictionary, and the response is a modified dictionary.

An even better example is the `Model.import_data()` method, which might ask `import_loop` to perform import conversion on a dictionary consisting of just one item.

Were it placed in the `Model` class, `import_loop` would essentially be a static method anyway. So I think the current arrangement seems pretty reasonable.

@martenson This is all a moving target right. There are several factors that prevent us from doing a perfect job at these things:

- Galaxy has many dependencies and isn't decomposed reasonably at all - it isn't even pip installable at this time. galaxy-lib sucks and it is the best we have done yet.
- Many projects such as ansible-galaxy-toolsand bioblend probably want to continue to target non-dev releases of Galaxy - this means not depending on having access to a ``GALAXY_ROOT``.

Within those restrictions though... a couple guidelines I would use, when I think about this and efforts such as breaking up Galaxy into pieces (https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/issues/1396 and galaxy-lib).

- Dependencies should be directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). So despite the comment by @martenson - bioblend should not depend on this! Galaxy and planemo can depend on this and they in turn can depend on bioblend - but we shouldn't be creating cycles. The principle applies at the project level, at the level of decomposing Galaxy into sub projects, and at the level of individual modules within any of these projects.
- Stuff in PyPI should have the correct dependencies. Since this project can't import ``galaxy.model`` I didn't think scripts that used it should be a part of this project - they would be broken by design. In https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/issues/1396 I made the case that as galaxy-lib materializes the next target should be "just enough" models to be dangerous. I should be possible to leverage the Galaxy data model without requiring web server components, a workflow runner, and IE backend.
- Once something that leverages the Galaxy models directly and can be used to read a "Galaxy configuration" in some fashion is in PyPI - one can imagine taking most of the database management stuff out of $GALAXY_ROOT/scripts as well as stuff like bootstrapping users and making a project out of it. This is very dependent on https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/issues/1396 however.
- I think as a principle - in general, it is better to develop smaller components that don't have deep dependencies outside of Galaxy core. This is the natural growth of large projects and keeps the design  decoupled as well as the administration. If apt-get install or pip install Galaxy should have access to this scripts - we can now pretty easily create a dependency.
- There have been efforts to keep bioblend fairly un-opinionated - it tries to be a fairly thin layer of the Galaxy API (e.g. https://github.com/galaxyproject/bioblend/pull/143) - this is why I put these scripts that I considered opinionated here instead of in bioblend. I didn't initially agree @nsoranzo on this - but I think now that I'm more comfortable with creating and maintaining projects - it seems like a decent design decision. Likewise, this is why gxformat2 is its own project (https://github.com/jmchilton/gxformat2).
- I think this project is the realm for things that manage Galaxy but don't require Galaxy - management that leverages the API. For the time being, things that require Galaxy and Galaxy dependencies - should still be placed in $GALAXY_ROOT/scripts.
- In general, projects should prefer the Galaxy API to the models and file system or database access to Galaxy, the models to filesystem or database access to Galaxy.

I guess all the same principles of isolation and decoupling and preferring interfaces to implementation, etc... apply at the level of the libraries just as much as at the level of class design.

This problem actually exists for historical reasons and is on its way out. The [ACME](https://github.com/ietf-wg-acme/acme) spec used to require that the content type be exactly `text/plain`. When this was true, the client behaved properly.

In the past couple weeks, this restriction has been removed from the spec and the client is in the process of being updated. Already merged into `master` is a fix so that the client will continue to pass the challenge response to `boulder` (the Let's Encrypt server) if it is unable to verify the challenge. This fix will be in place on PyPI later on today.

The client also no longer needs to verify `Content-Type` header and this check should be removed. It appears we currently don't have an issue for this problem so I'm hijacking this one. Please respond if you think there are other problems that should be addressed.

Unfortunately, I'm not sure if we'll ever make the suggested change here. Doing so would require some major rearchitecture of the client and would take some time. Furthermore, it also is likely to make things more complicated for us. For instance, when the certificate is renewed, we'd have to make sure that the symlinks for every domain in the certificate are updated. This leads to more problems like properly cleaning up the mess if we crash before updating all symlinks.

With that said, I can explain to you how the name is chosen as well as offer a potential solution to your problem. As you may already be aware, all domains that are input to a single run of `letsencrypt` are put into one certificate. The name of directory in `/etc/letsencrypt/live` is the first name you specify, whether it be on the command line or at the prompt given by LE if you do not specify the names on the command line. This is not necessarily true when using plugins like Apache which try to detect the domain names for you, but I don't believe this applies to you.

If the above information does not help, another thing you do is to run LE once for each domain. While this is less efficient, the directory is created in `/etc/letsencrypt/live` as you expect and may make things easier for automation purposes.

Hmmm..., wait. After some thought, wouldn't it simpler especially from client perspective if we just use `UpdatedAt` approach?

If we use `UpdatedAt` approach, client just simply store the last message of the timestamp for next request. On the next request client could just use this stored timestamp to fetch the latest messages including deleted messages. For every messages retrieved, client then just need to check the `DeletedAt` field. If it is nil, append to cache, but if it's not then delete from cache.

But if we used `DelID` approach, client also need to keep track of last `DelID` so it doesn't need to retrieve full list of deleted message ids every time it make request to server.

On the server side, if use UpdatedAt approach we just need to change a bit our adapter & keep existing architecture. But if we use `DelID` approach, we might change a lot existing architecture, not only just the adapter.

What do you think?

Dedicating a thread to `poll()` calls definitely works, but hinders scalability.

With one thread per broker, librdkafka is already very liberal in it's use of threads. This may be tolerable for most languages where you run one application process per host and maximize CPU utilization by running more worker threads. With Python and its GIL however you achieve better utilization by running more worker processes.

We typically run over a hundred worker processes per host. In the environment with 10 Kafka brokers we are looking at having a process go from one thread to 13 (main IOLoop thread, main librdkafka thread, 10 broker threads, polling and callback dispatch thread).

Napkin math:
* 100 processes * 12 threads = 1,200 more threads (context switching overhead)
* 1,200 threads * 2MB default stack size = 2.4 GB more RAM

Because of this cascading effect we will also have to keep in mind that whenever we change Kafka broker topology we need to either decrease the number of workers we run (not to run out of memory), or to increase it (to get better utilization).

My understanding is that broker threads mostly sit idle blocked on IO. If that's the case - having one thread manage all broker sockets via `poll` or `select` should not negatively affect the throughput.

Ideally with asyncio and Tornado you would not need extra threads at all. Sockets can be put in non-blocking mode and registered with IOLoop.

Sure, threading model is not something you can change easily, but it would be a great future improvement for Python clients.

You are using a daemon, but in place of the compiled powerline executable you use the slowest “python client” option. There are three: socat-based sh script, Python-based python script and a compiled C script. When installing with pip these are chosen automatically: should the compiler be found by distutils C variant will be used, should `socat` be found shell variant will be used, Python as the absolute fallback. Given that you installed from the official repository you need to ask whoever packaged your powerline version.

You should update your Python version. 2.4.3 is not supported and will never be because I can write code that works on Python 2.6, 2.7, 3.2…3.4, but not code that works on Python 2.5 and 3.x, not to mention 2.4 because only in Python-2.6 `__future__` imports and some other features were added to make 3.x+2.x-conformant code possible to write.

You can compile your own Vim and your own Python and install both to `$HOME/.local` (or any other directory, just make sure that dynamic linker knows where Python libraries are, on my system (Gentoo) this is controlled by `LDPATH` setting defined in `/etc/env.d/*`). This is possible without breaking yum, but there are good chances that yum will not break by updating Python because 2.x versions were keeping backwards compatibility.
It is in any case a bad idea to install anything using `sudo pip`, this way pip will install to locations which are supposed to be controlled by a system package manager. Use `pip install --user`.

I was reading the discussion on PR #174. Just to add some context and see if I understand well (the doc is not always very clear):

We have a `Python Object` that by means of an `Schema` instance we serialize (`dump`) into a `Serialized Representation`, that we can take the backwards process and deserialize (`load`) into a `Python Object`.

A `Schema` have `Fields` that knows how the data can be de/serialized. That `Fields` can take an `attribute` from a `Python Object` and serialize it into a `key/name` in a map of a `Serialized Representation` and back.


Some comments answering the questions:

- `attribute` should be kept to decouple the schemas from your Python models/objects. The `attribute` parameter represents the name of the attribute of the `Python Object` where the data should be serialized from / deserialize to. If not present (`None`) Marshmallow assumes that the object attribute have the same name as the schema field. Same current behavior.

- `load_from`, `dump_to` arguments should be consolidated into one that represents the `key/name` on the `Serialized Representation`, with the mirror behavior of `attribute` but for the `Serialized Representation` side: if is not present (`None`) Marshmallow should assume that the `key/name` is he same as the schema field name.

- If you still need different values in `load_from` and `dump_to` what you need is two different schemas

- A `key` name could be too terse without a clear doc on what it represents. Other names could be: `key_name`, `name` (JSON), `field` (OpenAPI), `field_name`, `field_key`, etc.

I usually work differently, that's why i added the [Protocol](https://pyserial.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyserial_api.html#module-serial.threaded) class and threading helper to pySerial itself. Such a class can then buffer and provide methods for commands with a real timeout. E.g. [ATProtocol.command](https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/examples/at_protocol.py#L78) (and there I also prefer exceptions on timeouts ;-)  This is more flexible and also makes it easy to handle different "streams" of data on the serial port, like logging or event messages in parallel to responses to commands. Most devices I work with have that property (that they may send data on their own, such as logging).

As you have discovered pyserial-asyncio has not been tested in this scenario. It looks like the uses of `self._serial.fd` by pyserial-asyncio should be replaced by `self._serial.fileno()` which is supported by both the regular Posix and socket based Serial implementations, although curiously, not by the RFC2117 implementation.

The environment variable `AWS_REGION` should be getting set when Awsume is run, if it finds a region in the given profile. Awsume unsets all AWS environment variables prior to setting them in order to maintain consistency within your environment under one profile. If Awsume does not find a region in the profile, then the `AWS_REGION` environment variable will not be set.

So first I'd make sure that you have the `region` set in your `~/.aws/config` file for the profile you're trying to use. If you have a region in your config file and the environment variable is still not being set, then it may be due to a caching issue. Awsume might have cache'd your credentials from that profile from when the region wasn't in the config profile, so it's using the cache'd credentials instead of refreshing it. You could try running `awsume <profile_name> -r` to force-refresh the credentials.

If you have the region set in your config profile, and you're sure that you're using refreshed credentials, then there is an issue with Awsume, and I'd like to resolve that for you.

What platform are you running Awsume in? Windows/Mac/Linux? And what shell do you use?

There have for a very long time been requests to support strings (and sequences/regular expressions).
The format is similar to QF_S format used in CVC4, which is not yet documented at a level for broad consumption. However, some users seem to have taken this up and now ask why Z3 doesn't understand  "String" and parse string literals. 

Z3 now parses ASCII string literals (other character formats will have to wait) and lets you represent formulas over this type. The SMT-LIB2.5 spec specifies string literals (and in some way supposes a corresponding sort).

If you don't use interpreted functions on String, then you should be able to get away with removing your declaration of "String" and then rely on the built-in version. If you are really angry (or others are), then I will hack the parser to ignore duplicate String declarations so that we can serve both those that use built-in strings and those who don't.

Z3's default behavior is to load all logics if no logic is set. It should not load strings if a supported logic is set using the command set-logic, for example (set-logic QF_ABV). This behavior is perhaps not SMT-LIB proper, but seems rather convenient to me.

A native solver for strings (sequences) is in progress (this week) and I am checking it in directly as 
either users have to deal with "String" being reserved going forward and they might as well if they integrate latest builds, or users can leverage SMT-LIB front-end for strings as is in the various stages of support. Other efforts to support strings have been taken in projects. The front-end support for strings can be leveraged by other than the native solver.

Z3 has several custom heaps (small object allocators and also paged memory).

> Is there any advantage to making it a property, so it is computed on the fly? As you mentioned, we might as well make it an attribute and set it in the constructor, just like `input_shape`.

The advantage is that you can't set it from the outside, so it's guaranteed to always be consistent with `input_shape`. Not many layers would be happy about somebody changing `input_shape` after construction, though, so it doesn't really matter. Also it would be the only property in the library, which would raise the question why we don't encapsulate other attributes as well. So I'm fine with just setting an attribute.

> I think it's okay for `InputLayer` to get `output_shape` as well. If anything, it should only have `output_shape` and not `input_shape`, since it does not have an incoming layer itself.

It doesn't have `input_shape` anyway, it only has `shape` and always needs special treatment for now. Afterwards, it would have `shape` and `output_shape`, unless we decide to drop `shape` (which we can iff we make `output_shape` an attribute and not a property).

@lfrank ``'ElectrodeTableRegion`` is a dataset, not a group. You should be able to extend it like any other dataset. In your example, this would mean instead of defining a ``NWBGroupSpec`` you would need to define a ``NWBDatasetSpec`` instead. You can then add attributes etc. to the dataset. However, since it is a dataset you cannot add sub groups, datasets, or links. Similarly, ``ElectrodeTable`` is also a dataset so you will also need to use ``NWBDatasetSpec`` when you want to extend the table.

def load(self, data):
    buf, self.memberList = StructReader(data), list()

     self.steamIdClan, self.chatFlags, self.enterResponse, self.numMembers

We're making a CLI tool for managing our local docker/kubernetes containers in development. We have several "deployments" for various projects, and one of the commands we're providing in our CLI lets developers run configured project-specific commands inside the container.

So, say I have a deployment named `ecommerce-backend`, and it has a project-specific command `loaddummyproducts`, then the command in our CLI could look like:

```
$ ourtool run ecommerce-backend loaddummyproducts
```

So, the tough part is when we're trying to autocomplete here:
```
$ ourtool run ecommerce-backend <TAB>
```

At that point, we want to _only_ show the project-specific commands as choices, based on the deployment argument before it (`ecommerce-backend`). We don't want to show project-specific commands from other deployments/projects. Having `args` in our `complete()` function makes this a breeze.

Hi!
Welcome on board!

I will try to answer your questions, let us know what you think and we can drill down into details :)

>   can we use PostGIS with Kinto ?

In short: you can use PostGIS in the Kinto database but the provided backend code does not use PostGIS.

You can enhance the default DB schema with your custom tables, views, columns, triggers or whatever, but our backend code won't be aware of it. Of course, you could implement your own geospatial backend, and even publish it as a package for the community if you like :)

May I ask: do you really need PostGIS or you just want to do geospatial queries on your data? For the latter, the [ElasticSearch approach](https://kinto.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tutorials/write-plugin.html) would really be the most efficient IMO :)

> could use a custom PostgreSQL schema ?

There are two strategies:

* you keep the provided Postgresql backend, and you define a trigger that will spread the JSON field into your typed fields
* you define your own Postgresql backend, and you store the data the way you want from the dict objects you'll receive in the Python code

Hope it clarifies a bit :)

I suspect that with our current implementation, you should issue `set_option` on the same `sensor` object used for streaming, otherwise it will not apply.

Yes, `rs_enable_motion_tracking` is responsible for activating the motion module on-board the device. One of the services it provides is producing shared and high-resolution timestamps for all component hooked-up to it. Usually, librealsense takes care of that and just drops the timestamps on the relevant frames. 
However, let's say you have an external device (like a compass) and you wish to synchronize it precisely with image and motion. You could connect your device to a one of two 3.5v GPIOs (exposed on the side of the camera case). Every time you signal it, you get a timestamp callback with a frame number, source ID and a timestamp. 
This would allow you to synchronize your compass events (presumably coming to you though I2C or some other method) with realsense data. 

Personally, I have used this feature for testing - hook up an Arduino to an LED and a ZR300 GPIO. Now every time you toggle the LED you know exactly when it actually showed up on stream and when it was supposed to show up (more or less up to +-2ms)

On Thu, Apr 12, 2018, 5:53 PM Alex Dunn <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> @WardLT <https://github.com/WardLT> that is a good point, with one
> correction: we need more than the composition to determine the maximum bag
> lengths, we need the structure. We basically need to
>
> (1) calculate the number of each kind of bond in each *structure* (the
> "bag length")
> (2) for each bond, select the largest bag length from among all fitted
> structures
>
> So I suppose that could be a todo for BoB: determine the bag lengths
> without doing the CM computation.
>
> However I also think just due to the nature of BoB, something in the
> spirit of 'fit_featurize' might be nice, because (a) the *number of
> features is variable* with the set of structures to be featurized but (b)
> 'fitting' on one set of structures and 'featurizing' on another set is
> probably going to screw up any BoB featurizer implementation. You'll
> probably always want to fit and featurize on the *same dataframe* in one
> operation. This is because, unlike BondFractions, where if a bond type was
> missing from the training set you could just ignore it or approximate it,
> with BoB there are also problems if any structure in the test set has a
> larger bag than the corresponding bag in the training set.
>
> Basically what I am saying is that in some circumstances, if you fit on
> one dataset and featurize a different one, you run into trouble whether you
> decide to correct it or not. If you don't correct it, you have errors
> often. If you do correct it, you will either have an incomplete BoB vector
> or you will have to refit BoB!

I am confused as to why this does not answer your question.

> I noted this text as well. However, draft-ietf-core-resource-directory does not specify how the source port is "assumed".

"How" is implementation specific and not related to the issue. Related part is that it is assumed (in case "con" is not present).

> As noted at the end, this use of source address and port relates to future interactions with the LwM2M Client. The text you copied here does not state any relationship between the Client and the service that the Client is registering/updating.

That is the problem. The registration itself is successful, but later on, when LwM2M server wants to contact LwM2M client (e.g. read resource value), it sends a request to LwM2M Client, using the connection information that was stored from registration request. In other words LwM2M server expects that the client is reachable back by the same address/port the registration came in. The transport layer is not split into two distinct parts (client and server), but is seen as a single endpoint having an address and port, which is used for bidirectional CoAP request communication. LwM2M Client/Server describe service functionality and should not be confused with CoAP client/server.

Hi RQ, I've generally seen the term PROMISCUOUS to mean no filtering at all: neither PANID nor short address nor extended address. I'd suggest the following naming:
* PHY_FILTER_MODE_NORMAL: Normal device filtering is in place, e.g. address and PANID.
* PHY_FILTER_MODE_NETWORK: Only PANID filtering is in place.
* PHY_FILTER_MODE_PROMISCUOUS: All channel traffic is passed up from the PHY.

I generally don't like having two ways of doing the same thing, but I like this.

I'm tempted to go further. An operation can have both a summary and a description. This fits nice with usual docstrings:

```py
def my_view(...):
"""Summary

Long description...
"""
```

We could use a parser that sets the content before the first empty line as summary, and the rest as description. And we could decide that a line starting with `---` marks the end of the summary/description section and the rest is ignored.

Doing this would be compliant with apispec's docstring parsing (which parses the YAML docstring after the `---` line). In fact, the whole summary/description parsing feature could be made available to apispec. Part of the needed code is in apispec already.

We'd use this parser to parse the docstrings for summary/description and the result would be overriden if those are specified using `@blp.doc`:

```py
    def parser(func):
        [...]  # Black magic going on
        return {'summary': '...', 'description': '...'}


    def store_method_docs(method, function):
        doc = parser(function)
        doc.update(getattr(function, '_apidoc', {}))
        [...]
```

What do you think?

Extending a Cmd2 object with a custom ``do_xxx`` method is trivial if you do it **before** you initialize the instance of the object:

```Python
from cmd2 import Cmd

def do_xxx(self, arg):
    """Help for xxx command."""
    print('Hello from xxx')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    Cmd.do_xxx = do_xxx
    app = Cmd()
    app.cmdloop()
```

It is also trivial to extend a Cmd2 object with a custom method **after** you initialize the object **if you don't care about that method appearing in the help menu**:

```Python
    import types
    app = Cmd()
    app.do_xxx = types.MethodType(do_xxx, app)
    app.keywords += 'xxx'
    app.cmdloop()
```

With that code, running the **xxx** command will work and getting help on it with **help xxx** will also work, but if you just do **help** you won't see ``xxx`` in the menu.

If you actually want to extend i with a custom do_xxx method at runtime **and** see that command appear in the help menu, then you would probably need to override the ``cmd.get_names()`` method from the built-in ``cmd`` module which ``cmd2`` extends.  You would need to override it in your custom class which derives from ``cmd2.Cmd``.

OK, I've reproduced this and think I understand the issue. tl;dr: workaround until there's a proper fix is to use `attach_inline_image_file(..., domain="example.jpg")` (where "example.jpg" is something that doesn't end in ".com").

Here's what I think is happening…

> The error appears only when I'm sending email from the live server

That's a really helpful clue. And you were on the right path with the image name being a problem:

* I'm guessing that your live server hostname (what you get when you type `hostname` in a shell) ends in ".com", but when you test with the sandbox you're on a hostname that ends in something else, like ".your-isp.net".

* Anymail creates an inline image's Content-ID with Python's [`make_msgid`](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/email.util.html#email.utils.make_msgid), which by default uses your system's hostname. So when it's working, the Content-ID looks like `<12345...@ip10-1-1-10.your-isp.net>`, but when Gmail blocks the message the Content-ID looks like `<12345...@example.com>`. Those are both valid Content-IDs, so none of this should matter, except that…

* Mailgun re-uses the Content-ID as the inline-image's *filename*. And [Gmail blocks](https://support.google.com/mail/answer/6590) attachment *filenames* ending in ".com". (Most other ESPs allow separate filenames and Content-IDs, or just don't specify any filename for an inline image attachment.)

I'm investigating whether there's some way for Anymail to give Mailgun a separate filename for the inline image, or somehow prevent it from using the Content-ID as the filename. (Also, this may be a recent-ish Gmail or Mailgun change. I tested inline images to my Gmail account when I first wrote the Mailgun backend, but that was a few years ago.)

In the meantime, a workaround is to pass a specific `domain` to `attach_inline_image_file`:

```python
        cid = attach_inline_image_file(
           message,
           os.path.join(settings.ROOT_DIR, "/invitations/static/photos/2018-05-01.jpg"),
           domain="example.jpg",   # <<< add this
        )
```

(The domain is used only to construct the Content-ID, and it doesn't really have to be a true domain. The intent is to minimize chance of Content-ID collisions between if someone forwards your message and attaches their own inline images.)


> `print(message.as_string())` ... error `'AnymailMessage' object has no attribute 'as_string'`

Sorry about that, I meant to type `print(message.message().as_string())`. (But it wouldn't have actually shown the problem, as it wouldn't show the Mailgun-specific reworking of the inline image attachment.)

We unfortunately don't support `mod_macro` VirtualHosts as of yet. With `TLS-SNI-01` (which is now deprecated) it was still possible to get the certificates using Apache when run with `certonly` verb.

That said, I do see some issues with the renewals that make me wonder how it could have worked previously;
There's a incompatible mix of authenticators and installers: (a: standalone, i: apache), (a: apache, i: apache), (a: apache, i: none), (a: manual, i: none)

Usually this isn't a problem, but after Apache authenticator or installer has been run, there is Apache process listening on ports 80 and 443. Standalone plugin cannot shut down Apache to take control over the ports it needs for completing the validation. 
Also manual plugin cannot automate the renewal when used without `--manual-auth-hook` script.

Regarding the issue with TLS-SNI renewal: I have a hunch that because  `mod_macro` directive `<Macro ssl $host>` makes this macro apply to the `TLS-SNI` validation requests SNI header, its content takes precedence over the actual `VirtualHost` Certbot Apache plugin creates to answer the challenge.

ATM we're generating our certificates (only domain.tld & www.domainlt.d) with the certbot script. After that we take them from path `/etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.tld/`. Problem here, path can also be something like `/etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.tld-0001/`. Then we get an old cert instead of the correct one. So we want to get the correct path via a command line tool e.g. get path for domains xyz.

Why we cannot use the `--renew-hook`? Just simple: sometimes we also try to create/renew certs if a valid cert already exists (user created one by its own in past, instead of using our plugin), so then the hook is not triggered.

I think I figured it out.

We create a [`timeout_task`](https://github.com/mozilla-releng/scriptworker/blob/master/scriptworker/task.py#L413) that calls [`max_timeout`](https://github.com/mozilla-releng/scriptworker/blob/master/scriptworker/task.py#L518), and include that in the [set of futures to `await`](https://github.com/mozilla-releng/scriptworker/blob/master/scriptworker/task.py#L417). Since `max_timeout` doesn't exit for 2 hours, we sit there waiting for it to [sleep the entire 2 hours](https://github.com/mozilla-releng/scriptworker/blob/master/scriptworker/task.py#L534) before [returning](https://github.com/mozilla-releng/scriptworker/blob/master/scriptworker/task.py#L535).
@palango  https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/2115 fixes that issue

But generally speaking, timeouts are a good idea as the `wait_for_*` will wait indefinitely for specific events. Removing the timeout means that the tasks will run for as long as event takes to be received and processed by the raiden node, which can have side effects such as having many tasks waiting for events that we potentially won't receive due to certain failures.

Hi,

What do you mean by ``Categories`` is not saved?
Do you get an error message, or no items in the database? How do you check for its existence?

A specific thing to know about Django's TestCase is that each test function runs in a transaction (see [the docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/testing/overview/#writing-tests)).
This means that, with your example:
1. The test database is created
2. ``channel`` is created in the database when loading the test code
3. A transaction begin before ``test_models``
4. The categories are created within that transaction
5. The transaction is rolled back, leaving only ``channel`` in the database.

Another notes:
- since you set ``channel_id = 1`` in your ``CategoryFactory``, this might conflict with passing ``channel=``: both would work about the foreign key; the outcome will depend upon Django's behavior here.
- Passing ``parent__parent__parent=None`` isn't needed when you provide an explicit value for ``parent``, unless that value is itself a factory-related declaration.

This is a clearly a case where the documentation (at least what's there so far) needs work. There are two ways to make this work:

1. Use '/home/jacob' as your top_level_directory_arg. It will then find the subdirectory of 'H5test' as a valid channel and you should be able to proceed.
2. Typically, we would use one further directory level to record data, using '/home/jacob/H5test/<channel_name>' in the Digital RF Sink block. Then we think of 'H5test' as the top-level experiment directory containing subdirectories of channel data. In that case, using '/home/jacob/H5test' as the top_level_directory_arg would be what works.

Let me know if either of those doesn't work!

hi @robertnishihara,
Google style organizes imports in 4 groups. 
Within each group, imports are sorted lexicographically.
See https://github.com/google/styleguide/blob/gh-pages/pyguide.md#313-imports-formatting.

And it uses `import x` to import single-level modules, and uses `from x.y import z` to import multi-level modules. 
See https://github.com/google/styleguide/blob/gh-pages/pyguide.md#224-decision 

I'll change the code according to the above rules.

Also, `import x.y` and `from x import y` should be equivalent in terms of how a module is imported by the interpreter. It only make differences in how you reference y. See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/710551/use-import-module-or-from-module-import
Thus regarding the issue you mentioned above, I don't think `from import` is the culprit. I didn't dig deep, it seems to me that the problem is circular imports.

Except when the URL expires as you need to do all the URL resolution upfront. 

If you are using linux you can do it with a simple bash script.

**streamlink-playlist.sh**
```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash

playlist=$(mktemp -t)
for url in $*; do
    streamlink --stream-url "${url}" best
done > ${playlist}

mpv --playlist=${playlist}
rm -f ${playlist}
```
and run it by doing 

```sh
bash streamlink-playlist.sh http://www.crunchyroll.com/girlish-number/episode-11-wavering-chitose-and-determined-gojo-722465 http://www.crunchyroll.com/girlish-number/episode-12-karasuma-chitose-and-722467
```

Just a thought:

- On embedded systems such as RPI, version-checking on startup isn't necessary unless something goes wrong or there's a security issue (which should probably be manually updated anyway). Overall, it just adds a delay to getting to the requested content every time it is called.
- On actively maintained systems, it's nice to know when there's an update, but if other applications depend on streamlink (such as streamlink-gui), they may not support the newest version yet. When they update, they can then run a version check on streamlink to see if a supported one is installed, if not, they can request the user for an updated version.

With this in mind,
What if we disabled the version check on startup on all platforms and if a plugin fails to tap into a stream or there's another error, it either:
A) Automatically runs a version check to see if there's an update to possibly fix the error. We can also spit out the currently installed version number to make it easier - just in case something goes wrong with the version check. Would need some kind of gated version checking so a quick succession of failures doesn't spam the update servers.
or
B) Let the end-user manually check for an update if there's an error tapping into the stream or another error, by suggesting to run <commands: pip/linux/windows/update website> to see if there's an update. We can also spit out the currently installed version number to make it easier.

Thoughts?

> effects, so you can quickly run python code just as always and ignore mypy

I had an idea.

# Simplified nurseries

We *could* make a version of nurseries where the code inside the `async with` block is treated as "just another supervised task". The semantics would just be, if a background task raises an exception, we immediately cancel the nursery *whether the parent task is in `__aexit__` or not*. Since the body of the `async with` is inside the nursery's cancel scope, this would immediately cancel the body as well, along with all the tasks.

This could even make some simple kinds of supervision patterns easier. For example, the root task (`init`) in trio has a special supervision pattern where it's just like a normal nursery, *except* that there's one special task (the "main" task) where if it exits normally, then we cancel everything, just like when any task raises an error. [Here's another case where this pattern comes up.](https://twitter.com/ramalhoorg/status/883096636993531905) With this "simplified" nursery design, this can just be written like:

```python
async def init_task():
    async with trio.open_simplified_nursery() as nursery:
        try:
            return await main_task_fn()
        finally:
            nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

This could be implemented on top of the current system by having a "simplified" nursery that immediately spawns a background task to watch for crashed children, which runs until the parent task hits `__aexit__`, at which point the background task goes away and the parent takes over again. (This is useful to have correct semantics for when the nursery should be closed -- you don't want the background task holding the nursery open after everything else has exited.) And the simplified nursery would only expose `spawn` and `cancel_scope` attributes – all the other stuff in the nursery interface is really just for custom supervisors.

Alternatively, if we baked this into the core, it could be even simpler: we could hard-code in the task exit path that when a task exits with an error, we immediately cancel the parent nursery, and that would avoid any need for background tasks etc. It could also do some auto-reaping, which basically would mean, we know that the only thing nurseries actually need to keep around is a list of raised exceptions, so we can throw away the rest of each child task object as it exits. This probably isn't a huge win in practice though because if a task raised an exception then the exception keeps its stack frames in memory, and the task object itself is not much beyond that. ...or, no, wait, it is important, because we have long-running nurseries that have an unbounded number of successful tasks cycle through them, so you can't buffer all those in memory forever. But that would all be doable; probably simpler implementation-wise than what we have now.

But.

The main problem with this design is that it really is simplified, in the sense that it's strictly less capable than what we have now: you can implement the simplified nursery on top of the original-flavor nursery, but not vice-versa. In particular, original-flavor nurseries allow you to implement arbitrary custom supervision policies, like "restart any tasks that exit", but simplified nurseries cannot; they just have the one hard-coded policy. We need a way to implement custom supervisors.

One tempting approach would be to have the "simplified" nursery be the default, and then if you want to implement a custom supervisor, you tell trio "hey I got this" and it disables the auto-reaping. Something like:
```python
async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
    with nursery.custom_supervision:
        ...
```
An elegant further tweak would be to have the `with nursery.custom_supervision` block return the object that gives you `monitor`, `zombies`, etc., instead of having them available on the nursery object itself.

One thing I don't much like about this is that in practice, I think you'd *always* want to put the `with nursery.custom_supervision` line as the very first one inside the nursery block. The problem is that until you call this, the default supervisor is in play, so if you spawn some child and it crashes before you enter the supervision block, whoops, everything just died before your supervisor could start handling it. In principle you could avoid this by just being careful never to `await` in between the start of the nursery block and the start of the `nursery.custom_supervisor` block, but that's error-prone and doesn't really have any advantages AFAICT. (Is there any case where you'd *want* the auto-reaping behavior to start out enabled, then be disabled? Seems pretty weird.)

The API also makes it look like you could put the `with nursery.custom_supervision` line inside a child task and have them do the supervising, but again this has a nasty race condition where if another task crashes before the supervising child gets scheduled, then everyone gets cancelled immediately, and this race condition is impossible to avoid if you're spawning multiple tasks at once.

So this all argues that we should merge the `open_nursery` and enablement-of-custom-supervision into a single operation, and then Guido's rule against constant bool arguments says that we should have `async with open_nursery() as nursery` and `async with open_supervised_nursery() as (nursery, supervisor)`. (Assuming we want to keep the `monitor` and other attributes off the `nursery` object, which seems wise.)

Internally hopefully these could share code, though?

We might also want to change the supervised nursery semantics a bit, like maybe you want the rule that if it hits `__aexit__` with tasks still pending then that's an error, the supervisor was supposed to handle that? But eh, probably we still want to handle the case where supervisor code has a bug and itself crashes, and probably the simplest way to do that is still to require that the supervisor code run in the main task? Downside: you then can't emulate a simplified nursery using a supervisor nursery, because the simplified nursery can handle running arbitrary code in the main task. (This is why I'm worrying about putting supervisor loops into a child task.)

But if you want to handle supervisor crashes, and you want the put the supervisor in a child task... you have a real chicken-and-egg problem. I don't think there's any way to do it except by specifically nominating the supervisor task when setting up the nursery:
```python
async with trio.open_nursery_with_background_supervision(supervisor_afn) as nursery:
    ...
```

Question: does putting supervisor code in a child task violate the no-secret-background-tasks design principle? It's not as bad as a *real* implicit background task because there is a clear point where it's spawned, when you're creating the supervised nursery. But concretely I'm thinking of code like this, where from the caller's perspective, you just open the special nursery, and this secretly spawns a task:
```python
@asynccontextmanager
async def open_my_special_nursery():
    async with trio.open_supervised_nursery() as (nursery, supervisor):
        nursery.spawn(supervision_loop, supervisor)
        try:
            yield nursery
        finally:
            ... do... something... to push any main task exception to supervisor code and otherwise let it know that exiting is ok
```
...this is also rather tricky, isn't it, with the exception handling and everything. Maybe it really is better to just mandate that custom supervisors claim the job that starts them, so you can't willy-nilly spawn things from the body, and instead write something like
```python
# On cancellation, raises Cancelled out of the nursery body, which triggers fallback cleanup
async def run_with_restarts_supervised(*thunks):
    restart_table = {}
    async with trio.open_supervised_nursery() as (nursery, supervisor):
        for thunk in thunks:
            task = nursery.spawn(thunk)
            restart_table[task] = thunk
        async for task_batch in supervisor.monitor:
            for task in task_batch:
                # obviously in a real version of this we'd want rate limiting etc., this is just a toy
                # plus some kind of logging of why the task exited
                thunk = restart_table.pop(task)
                replacement_task = nursery.spawn(thunk)
                restart_table[replacement_task] = thunk

# Usage
await run_with_restarts(thunk1, thunk2)
```

It would be kinda cool though to be able to do
```python
async with open_restart_nursery(**config) as nursery:
    nursery.spawn(some_fn, *args, restart_limit=10)
```
Oh, and of course, you need the nursery object to be able to do things like `start_tcp_server(nursery)`. So yeah, we really do need to be able to run stuff in the main task, don't we. And if the default nurseries can run arbitrary blocking code in the main task, it's pretty weird if custom ones can't :-(

I guess the designated task version would look like
```python
async def supervisor_afn(nursery, supervisor_controller):
    ...

async with trio.open_supervised_nursery(supervisor_afn) as nursery:
    ...
```
...and maybe there'd be some special way we report issues in the main task to the supervisor function?

Not that this makes any sense for a nursery that restarts child tasks, since you can't restart the main task.

Are there actually any other use cases for custom supervision logic?

Ignore and log errors? Can be done with a simplified nursery + a custom `spawn` that wraps child tasks in a `try`/`except Exception`. Or just not letting errors escape in the first place.

Googling for erlang custom supervisors:
* http://erlang.org/pipermail/erlang-questions/2009-January/041092.html (states the "parenting is a full time job" rule; see also the surrounding thread)
* It sounds like the main reason people write custom supervisors is when they have some per-task state they want to track across restarts, when they need to do rate limiting on process spawns, or when they need to cut out some of the OTP behavior to get more scalability.

Meh, lots of questions here. I'm going to sleep on it.
